event_handlers: onInit: Read lawyer_id from URL query string; if present, invoke getLawyerProfile(lawyer_id); on success, store response in shared page state; on failure, set an error flag for display.
onRetryClick: Re-invoke getLawyerProfile with the same lawyer_id when the user retries after a load error.

components.functionality: Displays core lawyer attributes needed for decision-making and comparison, including full_name, years_of_experience, hourly_rate, rating, num_reviews, practice_areas, specialties, and is_accepting_new_clients status.
event_handlers: onProfileDataChange: When profile-data-loader updates the Lawyer object, refresh displayed fields such as years_of_experience, hourly_rate, rating, and specialties.
onAcceptingNewClientsChange: When is_accepting_new_clients changes, notify booking-related components so they can enable or disable booking interactions accordingly.

components.functionality: Shows the lawyer’s primary_office details (name, address, phone, email) and provides a link to the full office details page.
event_handlers: onProfileDataChange: When profile-data-loader provides a Lawyer object that includes primary_office details, render office name, address lines, city, state, zip, and contact info.
onClickViewOfficeDetails: Navigate to office.html?office_id={primary_office.office_id} when the user clicks the office details link.

components.functionality: Provides navigation back to the list of lawyers for broader comparison and search.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate the browser to lawyers.html?view=our_lawyers.

components.functionality: Allows the user to select the consultation_type (phone, video, in_person) used for filtering availability slots and configuring the booking form.
event_handlers: onProfileDataChange: Initialize available consultation_type options from lawyer.consultation_types; if the URL parameter consultation_type is absent or not in the allowed list, select the first valid option and update the URL.

onConsultationTypeChange: When the user selects a different consultation_type, update the consultation_type URL parameter and notify availability-loader to refresh availability for the new consultation_type.

components.functionality: Retrieves AvailabilitySlot data for the current lawyer based on URL-controlled filters (consultation_type, start_date, end_date) by calling getLawyerAvailability.
event_handlers: onInitOrProfileReady: Once a valid Lawyer object with id is available and initial consultation_type/start_date/end_date are determined (from URL or defaults), call getLawyerAvailability(lawyer_id, consultation_type, start_date, end_date) and store the resulting AvailabilitySlot list.
onFilterChange: When consultation_type, start_date, or end_date URL parameters change, re-call getLawyerAvailability with updated parameters.
onReloadRequest: Allow manual reload (e.g., user clicks a refresh button) by calling getLawyerAvailability using the current filter state.

components.functionality: Displays a calendar view based on the loaded availability range, highlights days with available slots, and lets users select a date which is then used to filter visible time slots.
event_handlers: onAvailabilityChange: When availability-loader updates the list of AvailabilitySlot items, recompute which dates have availability and update calendar indicators.
onDateSelect: When the user selects a date, update the date URL parameter and notify time-slot-selector to filter slots for that particular day.

onNavigateCalendarRange: When the user navigates to a different calendar range (e.g., next or previous week/month), update start_date and end_date URL parameters to match the visible range, which triggers availability-loader to fetch slots for that range.

components.functionality: Shows a list of available time slots for the currently selected date and consultation_type and allows the user to choose a specific slot for booking.
event_handlers: onAvailabilityOrDateChange: When AvailabilitySlot data or the date URL parameter changes, filter the list to show only slots whose start_datetime falls on the selected date and whose status is 'available'.

onSlotClick: When the user clicks a slot, update the availability_slot_id (or slot_id) URL parameter with the clicked slot’s id, ensure the consultation_type filter matches slot.consultation_type (and update the URL if needed), and notify booking-form to bind to this slot.
onSlotStatusChange: When an AvailabilitySlot’s status or current_bookings changes (e.g., due to a booking confirmation), hide or mark non-available slots.

components.functionality: Collects client contact details (client_full_name, client_email, client_phone) and notes, binds to the selected availability slot and consultation_type, and submits a booking using createConsultationBooking.
event_handlers: onSelectedSlotChange: When availability_slot_id in the URL changes or when time-slot-selector notifies a new selection, load the corresponding slot from current AvailabilitySlot data and set the consultation_type field accordingly; enable the form when a valid slot is selected and the lawyer is accepting new clients.
onInputChange: Update internal form state as the user types client_full_name, client_email, client_phone, and notes; perform inline validation as needed.

onSubmit: Validate that lawyer_id (from URL), availability_slot_id (from URL), consultation_type (from selected slot), client_full_name, client_email, and client_phone are present and valid; if valid, call createConsultationBooking(lawyer_id, availability_slot_id, consultation_type, client_full_name, client_email, client_phone, notes).

onBookingSuccess: When createConsultationBooking returns success=true, store the booking object in page state, display the booking-confirmation-panel, optionally update URL parameters booking_id and booking_status=confirmed, and disable further submissions for that slot.
onBookingError: When createConsultationBooking fails or returns success=false, display an error message, keep user input in the form, and allow resubmission.

components.functionality: Shows a confirmation summary after a successful booking, including the key properties of the created ConsultationBooking and status messages.
event_handlers: onBookingStateChange: When booking-form receives a successful createConsultationBooking response, render the booking’s start_datetime, end_datetime, consultation_type, status, and id.

onInitWithBookingParams: If the page loads and booking_id and booking_status URL parameters are present, display a generic confirmation message indicating that a booking has been completed, even if the full booking object is not re-fetched.
onDismissConfirmation: When the user dismisses or closes the confirmation panel, clear any transient confirmation flags in the page state (but not necessarily the booking_id URL parameter).

components.functionality: Allows the user to add the displayed lawyer to their shortlist using addLawyerToShortlist and reflects the result in the button state and a status message.
event_handlers: onClick: Call addLawyerToShortlist(lawyer_id) using the current lawyer_id from the URL; disable the button while the request is pending.

onShortlistSuccess: When addLawyerToShortlist returns success=true, update the internal LawyerShortlist state with the returned shortlist, adjust the button label to indicate the lawyer is saved, and show the API’s success message.
onShortlistError: When addLawyerToShortlist returns success=false or errors, show the API’s message as an error and allow the user to retry.

components.functionality: Displays the lawyer’s years_of_experience in a consistent, clearly labeled format to support the user’s comparison of experience across multiple lawyers.
event_handlers: onProfileDataChange: When the Lawyer object is updated, extract years_of_experience and render it prominently; if data is missing or invalid, hide the badge or show a neutral state.

components.functionality: Coordinates detection and display of errors such as missing lawyer_id, failed getLawyerProfile calls, or empty availability results, and offers retry or navigation options.
event_handlers: onMissingLawyerId: When lawyer_id is absent from the URL on init, display an error message indicating that no lawyer was specified and show a button that navigates back to lawyers.html?view=our_lawyers.
onProfileLoadError: When profile-data-loader reports a failure, show an error explanation and expose a retry control that triggers profile-data-loader.onRetryClick.

onNoAvailability: When availability-loader returns an empty list for the selected consultation_type and date range, show a message indicating no slots are available and provide controls to adjust filters (e.g., change date range or consultation_type).

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with content centered within a max-width container. Navigation uses horizontal flex-like distribution; hero content appears centered within the central portion of the grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global header and full-width navigation, the page uses a hero-style lawyer profile section followed by a vertically stacked booking section. The hero mimics the courthouse hero: a full-width background (subtle legal/architectural imagery), with a centered vertical stack of the lawyer’s name/summary, experience badge, and main actions (booking + shortlist). At the very bottom of this hero, a thin full-width strip contains primary office details, echoing the practice-areas strip. Below this, a full-width booking section stacks the consultation-type filter, the calendar and time slot selector as a paired band, the booking form, and finally the confirmation/state panels.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global header and full-width navigation, the page uses a hero-style lawyer profile section followed by a vertically stacked booking section. The hero mimics the courthouse hero: a full-width background (subtle legal/architectural imagery), with a centered vertical stack of the lawyer’s name/summary, experience badge, and main actions (booking + shortlist). At the very bottom of this hero, a thin full-width strip contains primary office details, echoing the practice-areas strip. Below this, a full-width booking section stacks the consultation-type filter, the calendar and time slot selector as a paired band, the booking form, and finally the confirmation/state panels.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header + nav retain roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. The lawyer hero/profile section occupies about the next 40–50% of the viewport height on first load, giving strong visual prominence. The thin primary-office strip at the bottom of the hero takes ~5–8% of viewport height. The booking area (filter, calendar, slots, form) then occupies the remaining vertical space in stacked bands, each full width with content constrained to a centered max-width container (~1040–1200px), using 48–64px vertical padding for the hero and 32–48px for booking sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Information is moderately dense, especially in the hero header area (name, credentials, experience badge, shortlist, primary office strip) and booking tools (filter, calendar, slots, form). Vertical spacing around key elements uses 16–24px increments, keeping the layout structured but not airy. Interactive controls (buttons, filters, date cells) are clearly separated using 8–16px gaps, maintaining a professional, information-forward aesthetic rather than minimalist emptiness.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left with the global logo, moves across the top bar to contact info, then down to the centered main navigation. From there, attention drops into the hero region: first to the back-to-results link at the top-left of the hero, then to the large, high-contrast lawyer name and profile summary in the middle. The experience badge near the name and the saturated primary booking CTA and shortlist button draw the eye next. The gaze then travels downward to the thin primary-office strip, and continues into the booking section: first the consultation-type filter, then across the horizontal band of calendar (left) and time slots (right), and finally down to the booking form and the confirmation/status panel. Loaders and error states sit centered over these regions, temporarily taking visual precedence when active.

layout.overall_description: The page sits beneath the site’s existing black header and deep red navigation bar. Immediately below the nav, a full-width hero-style section introduces the lawyer: a darkened, architectural or courtroom-inspired background spans edge-to-edge, with the main content centered both horizontally and vertically within a max-width container. At the top-left of this hero area is a subtle back-to-results link, while in the center a stacked cluster presents the lawyer’s name, title, and key summary details, accompanied by an experience highlight badge and prominent actions such as booking and saving to a shortlist. This cluster occupies the central horizontal band of the hero, leaving generous negative space above and below for the background imagery, mirroring the original courthouse hero. At the very bottom of the hero, a thin, full-width strip with a contrasting background color contains the primary office information, visually similar to the original practice-areas ribbon and acting as a base for the hero. Below this, the booking section fills the rest of the page in vertically stacked, full-width bands: first a compact consultation-type filter bar, then a more substantial band that horizontally pairs the availability calendar and time-slot selector within a centered container. Beneath that, the booking form spans most of the container width, and any booking confirmation or status panel appears directly below or overlaying this area. Loaders fade over their corresponding sections when data is being fetched, and the error state can replace the hero and booking content with a focused message. Overall, the layout preserves the mockup’s classic, corporate legal feel: strong top structure, a dominant central hero, high-contrast CTAs, and a clear, top-down Z-pattern flow through the booking tools.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-data-loader appears as a semi-transparent dark overlay covering the entire hero-profile region, from just below the red main navigation down to the top of the primary-office strip. It is full-width and matches the hero’s height (roughly 40–50% of the viewport on initial load). At its center, a spinner or loading indicator is placed, visually centered within the hero’s max-width container so it aligns with where the lawyer-profile-summary will appear. Underlying content is slightly dimmed but still visible, reinforcing that this loader is tied to the profile area specifically.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays and blocks interaction with the lawyer-profile-summary, shortlist-action-button, experience-highlight, and primary-office-section within the hero. It is non-sticky and scrolls with the page; it appears and disappears based on loading state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The lawyer-profile-summary is the focal element inside the hero section. It sits within a centered max-width container, horizontally centered on the page and vertically positioned in the central band of the hero so that there is roughly equal vertical space above and below it (about 48–64px padding to the hero edges). The summary content forms a vertical stack aligned slightly left-of-center inside the container: the lawyer’s name in large, bold, high-contrast type at the top, followed by their title/specialization and key summary information below, each separated by 8–16px gaps. The experience-highlight badge and shortlist-action-button are positioned to the right of or just below the primary text block, forming a balanced cluster. The entire summary block spans about 60–70% of the container width, leaving generous left and right margins for the background image to show, in line with the original hero’s central text treatment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section, scrolling away with the page. No sticky behavior; it anchors the hero’s visual center.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-office-section is implemented as a thin, full-width strip at the very bottom of the hero, echoing the practice-areas strip from the mockup. It spans edge-to-edge horizontally and is about 5–8% of the viewport height, with 16–24px vertical padding inside. A contrasting background (e.g., a deep red or muted gold band) differentiates it from the darker hero image. Within a centered max-width container, the office details are laid out in a single horizontal line or two compact rows: office name, address, and a subtle office-related icon or label, separated by small bullet-like dividers or vertical rules. The text is centered or slightly left-aligned within the container, but the band itself visually anchors the bottom of the hero section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, attached to the bottom edge of the hero section. It scrolls away with the hero as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-results-link is positioned at the top-left corner of the hero section’s content area, directly below the red main navigation bar. It sits within the same max-width container as the profile summary but aligned to the far left, with approximately 16–24px top margin from the hero’s upper edge and 8–16px left padding from the container edge. The link is styled as small, uppercase or semi-uppercase text with a subtle icon (e.g., a left arrow), and it occupies only as much width as needed, leaving a clear horizontal gap before the central profile summary content below. It aids orientation without competing visually with the central profile text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static link anchored near the top of the hero content; it scrolls normally with the page and does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation-type-filter sits immediately below the hero and primary-office strip as the first element of the booking section. It is contained within a full-width band with a solid background (typically light, contrasting with the darker hero) and 24–32px vertical padding. Within a centered max-width container, the filter controls (e.g., consultation type buttons, in-person vs. virtual toggles) are arranged horizontally across the center of the band, with each filter control having 12–16px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, mirroring the navigation item spacing from the mockup. The filter row spans approximately 60–70% of the container width, centered with comfortable side margins, and there is 16–24px spacing between this bar and the availability calendar section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal filter bar that scrolls with the page; no sticky behavior by default, though it visually functions like a secondary nav for the booking tools.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay spanning the entire booking-availability band that contains both the availability-calendar and time-slot-selector. It covers the full width of the container area dedicated to these components and extends from the top of the calendar/time-slot band down to just above the booking-form section. A centered loader indicator (spinner or progress icon) sits at the midpoint between the calendar and slots, aligned centrally in the page. Underlying calendar and slot content is blurred or dimmed slightly, reinforcing that the loader is specific to availability data rather than the whole page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays and blocks interaction with the availability-calendar and time-slot-selector while active. It scrolls with the page and appears/disappears based on the availability loading state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-calendar occupies the left side of a wide, full-width booking-availability band located directly beneath the consultation-type-filter. Inside a centered max-width container, the band is divided into two columns: the calendar on the left (~55–60% of the container width) and the time-slot-selector on the right (~40–45%). The calendar sits within a card-like panel with a subtle border or shadow, 24–32px internal padding, and 24–32px top/bottom spacing from neighboring elements. Its header (current month and navigation arrows) aligns at the top-left of the left column, with the date grid filling most of the card’s height. The card height is balanced with the time-slot panel on the right so the two appear as a cohesive horizontal pair, matching the mockup’s tendency to cluster key actions in a central horizontal band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within its booking band, scrolling with the page. It shares vertical alignment with the time-slot-selector to form a unified interactive row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-slot-selector is positioned immediately to the right of the availability-calendar within the same booking-availability band. Inside the max-width container, it occupies roughly 40–45% of the width, aligned flush to the right column. It also sits in a card-like panel with a matching border or shadow treatment and 24–32px internal padding, ensuring visual parity with the calendar. The top of this panel aligns horizontally with the top of the calendar card, and the bottom edges line up to create a uniform horizontal band. Time slots are displayed as a vertical or grouped list of buttons, each with 12–16px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, and 8–12px spacing between slots. The list is scrollable within the card if it exceeds available height, while the card itself maintains consistent height in relation to the calendar for a balanced composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within its column, scrolling with the page. Internal content (the list of slots) can scroll independently if needed, but the panel itself remains fixed in height relative to the calendar panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-form sits directly below the calendar-and-slots band, separated by 32–40px of vertical space. It spans most of the max-width container (approximately 70–80% of the container width) and is centered horizontally on the page. The form is presented in a structured, card-like panel with 32–40px internal padding and clear section breaks (e.g., personal details, case summary) using 16–24px vertical spacing between groups. On larger screens, form fields can be arranged in two columns within the panel, but the panel itself remains part of a full-width section with ample left/right margins. A primary booking submission button is placed at the bottom-right of the form panel, visually aligned under the main column of inputs, and given substantial padding (24–32px horizontal, 12–16px vertical) to align with the mockup’s CTA styling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form panel that scrolls with the page. It anchors the lower part of the booking section and provides a stable target for the booking flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-confirmation-panel appears in close proximity to the booking-form. On submission success, it either replaces the lower portion of the booking-form panel or appears immediately below it within the same max-width container, with 24–32px vertical spacing from the form. The panel spans roughly 60–70% of the container width and is centered horizontally, with 24–32px internal padding. It uses a slightly more prominent border, color accent (such as a muted gold or green success accent), or subtle overlay style to draw attention without overwhelming the page. Key confirmation details (date, time, consultation type, office) are stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing, echoing the direct, information-first style of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the booking section flow, scrolling with the page. Depending on UX decisions, it may temporarily overlay or collapse the form area, but it stays within the main content column rather than becoming a floating modal.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shortlist-action-button is placed within the hero’s central content cluster, closely associated with the lawyer-profile-summary. It typically appears to the right of the main profile text block, either on the same horizontal line as a primary booking CTA or directly below it, with 8–16px horizontal or vertical spacing. The button uses a noticeable accent color (potentially a rich cyan or lighter gold) and generous padding (24–32px horizontal, 12–16px vertical), visually echoing the high-saturation CTAs from the mockup. It does not span the full width; instead, it is a compact, clearly delineated control sitting within the central 60–70% of the container, aligning with the profile summary and experience-highlight badge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the hero’s content block, scrolling away with the hero. It does not stick to the viewport but remains visually prominent while the hero is in view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The experience-highlight appears as a compact info badge near the top of the lawyer-profile-summary block, typically positioned just below or to the right of the lawyer’s name. It is aligned with the main text column and sits inside the same central container, with 8–12px spacing from adjacent text elements. The badge uses a small but bold styling—perhaps a gold or deep red background with uppercase, white text—to stand out without overshadowing the name. Its width is limited to the content it contains, forming a pill or tag shape, and it reinforces the authoritative tone by emphasizing years of experience or specialization.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored to the profile summary content within the hero. It scrolls with the hero and does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The profile-error-state occupies the main content area that would otherwise hold the hero profile and booking sections. When triggered, it appears just below the red navigation bar and spans the full width of the viewport, with a centered max-width container holding the error message. The error panel itself is vertically compact but visually strong, with 32–40px padding and about 16–24px margin above and below, and it may use a dark red or muted alert background consistent with the site’s color palette. The message is centered or left-aligned within the container, and a link or button to go back to results or retry loading is placed beneath the message with 16–24px spacing. In this state, the usual hero profile content and booking tools are either hidden or visually de-emphasized beneath the error panel, keeping focus on resolving the problem.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static alert panel that replaces or sits above the main page content when active. It scrolls with the page but typically appears near the top so users see it immediately on load.

name: Resources & Articles
title: Resources & Articles
description: Search, filter, and browse legal resources (articles, guides, FAQs) by topic, keyword, time range, and sort order, with quick access to saved items in your reading list.

core_features: Content-type switching to filter resources by type (e.g., articles, guides, FAQs), driven by available content types from getArticleSearchFilters and URL parameters (content, content_type).
Keyword search over resource titles and content using searchArticles, synchronized with the query URL parameter.
Topic and time-range filtering using options from getArticleSearchFilters, synchronized with topic and time_range URL parameters.
Sorting of search results using sort options from getArticleSearchFilters, synchronized with the sort_order URL parameter.
Search results listing using searchArticles, displaying article title, summary, publish date, topic, tags, content type, read time, and linking to article detail pages.
Reading list awareness via getReadingList: highlighting which search results are already saved and providing a quick access list of saved resources.
URL-driven state so that all search, filters, and sort settings are shareable and restorable when reloading or navigating back to the page.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Landing on the Resources page from the header
- Step 1: User clicks the "Resources" navigation link in the header.
- Step 2: Browser navigates to resources.html with a content URL parameter preset to a specific content tab.
- Step 3: On page load, the controller component calls getArticleSearchFilters.
- Step 4: The controller determines the initial content_type from the URL (content_type or content) and validates it against the content_types from getArticleSearchFilters.
- Step 5: The controller reads query, topic, time_range, and sort_order from the URL (if present) and validates them against the filter options.
- Step 6: The controller calls searchArticles with the effective query, topic, time_range, sort_order, and content_type.
- Step 7: In parallel, the controller calls getReadingList to retrieve the user's saved articles.
- Step 8: The page renders: content-type tabs, search form, filter bar, sort controls, and the results list, with reading list indications applied.

Workflow 2: Running a keyword search and refining by topic/time range
- Step 1: User types a term in the search input and submits the form.
- Step 2: The search-form component updates the query URL parameter (preserving any existing topic, time_range, sort_order, and content_type) and navigates to the updated resources.html URL.
- Step 3: The page controller re-reads the URL parameters and re-runs searchArticles with the new query and existing filters.
- Step 4: Results update to show only resources matching the keyword.
- Step 5: User selects a topic from the topic filter dropdown; the filter-bar component updates the topic URL parameter (preserving other parameters) and triggers navigation.
- Step 6: Page controller re-runs searchArticles with the new topic filter, and results refresh.
- Step 7: User selects a time range; the filter-bar updates the time_range URL parameter, triggers navigation, and searchArticles is called again to refresh results.

Workflow 3: Changing content type and sort order
- Step 1: User clicks another content-type tab (e.g., switching from articles to guides).
- Step 2: The content-type-tabs component updates the content_type URL parameter (and optionally the content parameter for consistency), preserving query, topic, time_range, and sort_order, then navigates to the updated URL.
- Step 3: Page controller validates the new content_type against available content_types, then calls searchArticles with the updated content_type and current query/filters.
- Step 4: User opens the sort dropdown and selects a different sort option.
- Step 5: The sort control updates the sort_order URL parameter and navigates.
- Step 6: Page controller re-runs searchArticles using the new sort_order so results reappear in the selected order.

Workflow 4: Viewing a specific article from search results
- Step 1: From the results list, user clicks on a resource title or its card.
- Step 2: The article-results-list component reads the selected Article.id and navigates the browser to article.html with article_id set to that id.
- Step 3: The article detail page uses the article_id parameter (out of scope here) to load and display the full content.

Workflow 5: Using the reading list while browsing search results
- Step 1: On load, the controller calls getReadingList and receives a list of Article entities currently saved in the user's reading list.
- Step 2: The article-results-list component compares each search result Article.id with the article_ids in the reading list data and marks matching items as "Saved" or similar.
- Step 3: The reading-list-strip component displays a compact list of saved articles returned from getReadingList, each linking to article.html with the appropriate article_id.
- Step 4: User clicks a saved resource in the reading-list-strip; the component navigates to article.html with article_id set for the selected saved article, allowing quick access from any search state.

Workflow 6: Sharing or revisiting a filtered resources view
- Step 1: After refining filters and search terms, user copies the current URL from the browser address bar (which includes query, topic, time_range, sort_order, and content_type parameters).
- Step 2: The URL is shared or saved.
- Step 3: When the URL is opened again, the page controller parses the parameters, validates them against getArticleSearchFilters, and calls searchArticles with identical parameters.
- Step 4: The page reconstructs the exact same search, filters, and content-type selection so the recipient sees the same resource set.
interactions: Typing in the keyword search field and submitting triggers an update to the query URL parameter and a new searchArticles call.
Clearing the keyword search (e.g., submitting an empty search) removes or empties the query parameter and re-runs searchArticles with other filters unchanged.
Clicking a content-type tab updates the content_type (and optionally content) URL parameter and re-runs searchArticles with the new content type.
Selecting a topic from a dropdown or similar control updates the topic URL parameter, then refreshes results via searchArticles.
Selecting a time range option updates the time_range URL parameter and refreshes results via searchArticles.
Choosing a sort option updates the sort_order URL parameter and refreshes results via searchArticles.
Clicking an article title or card in the results navigates to article.html with article_id set to that article's id.
Clicking a saved item in the reading list section navigates to article.html with article_id set to that saved article's id.

Loading or reloading the page (including using the browser back/forward buttons) triggers re-parsing of URL parameters and re-fetching of filters (getArticleSearchFilters), results (searchArticles), and reading list (getReadingList).
page_functionality.state_logic: On each page load or URL change, the page controller parses the URL parameters content, content_type, query, topic, time_range, and sort_order. First, getArticleSearchFilters is called to retrieve the lists of topics, content_types, time_ranges, and sort_options. The effective content_type is determined by prioritizing a valid content_type parameter if it matches one of the content_types returned by getArticleSearchFilters; if not present or invalid, the content parameter is checked against the same list; if still invalid or absent, the controller uses a fallback content_type from the returned content_types (for example, the first or a designated default). The effective topic, time_range, and sort_order are determined by validating their URL parameter values against the corresponding arrays from getArticleSearchFilters; invalid values are ignored so that those parameters are effectively unset when calling searchArticles. The effective query is taken directly from the query URL parameter (or treated as empty if missing). Once all effective values are resolved, searchArticles is called with these parameters. In parallel, getReadingList is invoked without parameters to fetch the current reading list. All visible state (active tab, filled search field, selected filters, selected sort, results list, and saved-status indicators) is derived from these API responses and the resolved URL-backed state, with no separate persistence in localStorage or other client storage. Any user interaction that changes search terms, filters, sorting, or content type updates the corresponding URL parameters and triggers the same resolution and re-fetch sequence, ensuring the URL is always the single source of truth for the page's state.

components.functionality: Central controller that initializes the page, manages URL-driven state, calls getArticleSearchFilters, searchArticles, and getReadingList, and distributes data and state to child components.

Displays a tab for each available content type (from getArticleSearchFilters.content_types) and lets the user switch between resource types, updating the content_type URL parameter (and optionally content) to drive searchArticles.
Provides a single keyword input bound to the query URL parameter and triggers a new search when submitted.
Renders topic and time-range filters using options from getArticleSearchFilters and ensures the selected values are synchronized with topic and time_range URL parameters.
Displays available sort options from getArticleSearchFilters.sort_options and updates the sort_order URL parameter when the user selects an option.
Shows the total number of results returned by searchArticles and reflects the current query and filters in a concise summary.

Displays the list of Article items returned from searchArticles, including each resource's key fields (title, summary, topic, publish_date, tags, content_type, read_time_minutes, author_name when available) and indicates whether each article is present in the current reading list.
Shows a compact, navigable list of articles from getReadingList so users can quickly access already-saved resources while browsing search results.
Displays a message and guidance when searchArticles returns zero results for the current query and filters, suggesting the user adjust or clear some filters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with content centered within a max-width container. Navigation uses horizontal flex-like distribution; hero content appears centered within the central portion of the grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global site chrome (black utility header + red primary navigation + hero banner), the Resources & Articles page content follows the same linear, top-to-bottom stack with no sidebars. Within the main content area, components are ordered from high-level page controls down to detailed content: first the page controller (title and high-level actions), then content-type tabs, then the search form, followed by a compact filter + sort toolbar, then the results summary and main results list. The reading list sits as a persistent, thin strip anchored at the bottom of the viewport, visually echoing the practice-areas strip in the hero. The empty state replaces the results list region when no content is available.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global site chrome (black utility header + red primary navigation + hero banner), the Resources & Articles page content follows the same linear, top-to-bottom stack with no sidebars. Within the main content area, components are ordered from high-level page controls down to detailed content: first the page controller (title and high-level actions), then content-type tabs, then the search form, followed by a compact filter + sort toolbar, then the results summary and main results list. The reading list sits as a persistent, thin strip anchored at the bottom of the viewport, visually echoing the practice-areas strip in the hero. The empty state replaces the results list region when no content is available.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Mirroring the mockup, the top 15–20% of the main content area is a relatively compact band dedicated to the page controller, tabs, search, and filters (dense but orderly). The central 60–70% of the content height is allocated to the results list or empty state, forming the core browsing area. A narrow 5–10% horizontal strip at the bottom of the viewport is reserved for the reading-list component, full-width but visually lightweight. Horizontally, all content is centered within a max-width container (e.g., ~1140–1200px) on a 12-column grid; components typically span 8–12 central columns to maintain the focused, authoritative feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout uses a moderately dense information pattern consistent with a corporate legal site. The top controller, tabs, and filter bar are relatively compact: small vertical spacing (16–24px) between elements, closely grouped controls, and minimal decorative whitespace. The search bar and main results list use slightly more breathing room (24–32px between sections, 24px+ padding within cards) to enhance scannability. Overall, the layout favors clearly segmented bands of content rather than airy, minimalist expanses.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is guided in a top-down Z-pattern that echoes the mockup. Within the main content, attention starts at the top-left with the page title in the page controller, moves rightward across any high-level actions, then drops down to the centered content-type tabs. From there, the user’s gaze falls naturally to the prominent, centrally aligned search bar, then sweeps left-to-right across the filter bar and sort control. The results summary acts as a small anchor at the top of the results area, leading into the vertically stacked list of articles. Finally, the eye settles onto the bottom reading-list strip, which acts as a persistent anchor and a subtle secondary focus similar to the hero’s practice-areas strip.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s black utility header, red navigation, and hero banner, the Resources & Articles page content sits in a centered, max-width container on a 12-column grid. At the very top of this page section, a firm, left-aligned page title bar establishes context, with supporting actions aligned to the right, creating a strong horizontal line. Directly beneath it, a row of bold, all-caps tabs stretches across the center, echoing the style of the main navigation but scaled slightly down. A large, single search bar then occupies the central width of the page, serving as the primary interaction focal point, framed by generous top and bottom padding. Just below, a compact horizontal filter bar hugs the left side while the sort dropdown anchors the right, forming a tight control band. Under this band, a low-profile results summary introduces the main content region, which is dominated by vertically stacked article cards that fill the central portion of the viewport. Each card spans most of the content width, creating a solid, columnar block of information in the middle of the page. Across the very bottom of the viewport, a thin, full-width reading-list strip sits persistently, visually similar to the hero’s practice-area strip: dark, saturated background, compact text, and a clear call-to-action, acting as a subtle but constant reminder of saved content. When there are no results, the central card area is replaced by a centered empty-state panel that occupies the same vertical band, while the persistent bottom reading-list strip remains available.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller sits at the top of the Resources & Articles content area, directly below the hero/banner region and aligned with the global content container. It forms a horizontal bar occupying the full width of the central 12-column grid, with approximately 24–32px of top padding from the hero’s bottom edge and 16–24px of bottom padding to the tabs. On desktop, the page title (e.g., “RESOURCES & ARTICLES”) is left-aligned, spanning roughly the left 6–8 columns, set in a bold, all-caps or small caps style to echo the site’s authoritative tone. On the right, using the remaining 4–6 columns, secondary actions (e.g., a subtle link to ‘Manage Reading List’ or filter presets) align horizontally, forming a balanced left–right composition similar to the top header with logo left and contact info right. The background remains the main page background (likely light or subtly textured) to keep this band visually distinct from the darker hero above and the more content-heavy area below. The controller is compact and dense: minimal vertical height, with elements tightly arranged but clearly separated by ~8–16px internal spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in normal document flow; it scrolls away with the page content and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Immediately beneath the page controller, the content-type tabs form a full-width horizontal row centered within the same max-width container. Vertically, they sit about 8–16px below the bottom edge of the page controller, creating a clear but tight separation. Horizontally, the tab labels are distributed across the central 8–10 columns, with equal spacing between them, producing a navigation-like band reminiscent of the main red nav bar but visually lighter (e.g., white or light background with dark text and a gold or deep red active underline). The tabs are aligned in a single row, centered on the page; if there are many tabs, they are still constrained to one line in desktop view, using slightly condensed spacing. Each tab has approximately 12–16px horizontal padding and 8–10px vertical padding, matching the navigation item rhythm from the mockup. The active tab stands out with a bold weight and/or colored underline spanning its label width, anchoring the user’s attention before moving down toward the search. Small vertical padding of ~16–24px above and below the tab row creates the sense of a distinct band between the page controller and the search form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard horizontal row in document flow; tabs do not stick but scroll with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form sits directly beneath the content-type tabs as the primary functional focal point of the page. It is centered horizontally within the max-width container and spans roughly 60–70% of the page width (about 7–9 of the 12 grid columns). There is generous vertical spacing: about 24–32px above it (from the bottom of the tabs) and 24–32px below it (before the filter bar), making this band feel slightly more open compared to the dense header and filter areas. The search input itself is a single, wide text field with a prominent height (40–48px), flanked on the right by a dark, high-contrast submit button (deep red or black with light text) that echoes the site’s CTA style. The input and button form a single horizontal unit, with the input occupying roughly 80–85% of this band’s width and the button the remaining 15–20%. The entire form is centered, with symmetrical left/right margins to reinforce balance and authority. On larger screens, the field is large enough to visually echo the hero’s central CTAs, effectively becoming the ‘hero control’ of this page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element; remains in normal flow and scrolls out of view as the user moves down the results list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is positioned immediately below the search form, forming a compact, horizontally oriented control band. It begins about 16–24px under the search form’s bottom edge to maintain a sense of connection while still being visually distinct. Within the 12-column grid, the filter controls occupy the left 7–8 columns, left-aligned to the content container. The bar may contain multiple filter controls (e.g., topic dropdown, date range, jurisdiction), arranged left-to-right in a single row where space allows, with 16–24px gaps between individual filter inputs or pills. The overall height of this band is relatively short, perhaps 48–64px including padding, preserving the moderately dense feel typical of professional services sites. The background remains neutral (same as page background or a slightly tinted panel) to avoid competing with the more prominent central search bar. On narrower viewports, filters may wrap to a second line, but still remain visually grouped as one band above the results summary. The filter bar sets up the left anchor in the left–right balance with the sort control on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flow element. Optionally, on longer-scrolling lists, this bar could be implemented as sticky within the content area (sticking to the top of the viewport once scrolled into position) but visually it should still appear as part of the normal stacked layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is visually paired with the filter bar, aligned on the same horizontal axis but anchored to the right side of the content container. On desktop, it sits in the rightmost 3–4 columns of the same row as the filter bar, vertically aligned to the center of the filter controls. The dropdown label (e.g., “Sort by:”) and the select element are compact, with approximately 8–12px padding inside and 8–16px margin to the container edge, matching the tight layout style of the header’s right-aligned utilities. This creates a clear left–right tension similar to the global header: filters on the left, sorting on the right, forming a balanced, slightly compressed control band. On smaller screens, the sort control drops below the filter bar and becomes left-aligned, but still separated by a small 8–12px vertical gap. The sort control’s visual weight is lighter than that of the search field but heavier than the results summary, making it easily discoverable without overpowering the main search.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as part of the same row as the filter bar in normal document flow; on desktop it aligns to the right, on mobile it stacks under the filters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary is positioned directly under the filter/sort band, acting as a subtle transition into the main results area. It sits approximately 12–16px below the bottom edge of the filter bar (or sort control, whichever is lower), maintaining the moderately dense vertical rhythm. The summary text (e.g., “Showing 24 results for ‘employment law’”) is left-aligned and spans roughly the left 6–8 columns of the grid, with no heavy visual styling—regular weight, small-to-medium type size—to prevent it from competing with the article titles below. The summary’s vertical footprint is small, essentially a single text line with maybe 4–8px of padding above and below, letting it function as a label for the content block that follows. On the right side of the same row, a small ‘clear filters’ link or chip could appear, but the main layout assumption is a single-line summary that ties the controls above to the results list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level text element in normal flow; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article results list occupies the central, largest portion of the page height, directly below the results summary. It spans the full width of the central content container, typically using the central 8–10 columns for actual card content with 1–2 columns of side padding or margin on either side to maintain a solid, columnar appearance. Each article entry is presented as a horizontal card or row, with a consistent vertical stacking: title at top, metadata (date, category, reading time) below, followed by a short excerpt and a subtle ‘Read more’ link. Each card has 16–24px internal padding and is separated from the next card by 16–24px vertical spacing, preserving a structured, moderately dense but readable list. The list extends downward, potentially beyond the viewport, encouraging vertical scrolling similar to how the hero allows vertical breathing for the image. The visual emphasis is on strong, left-aligned titles with dark text, mirroring the bold, white hero tagline but adapted to a light background. The entire list forms a visual ‘column’ that anchors the page center, with the reading-list strip at the bottom acting as a counterbalancing band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content in document flow; the list simply grows in height, with no fixed positioning. Pagination or infinite scroll controls, if any, appear at the bottom of this list area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list component appears as a narrow, full-width strip pinned to the bottom of the viewport, visually echoing the practice-areas strip at the bottom of the hero. It spans 100% of the browser width but its internal content is constrained to the same max-width central container. The strip is relatively thin, occupying roughly 5–10% of the viewport height, with vertical padding of about 12–16px and a dark, high-contrast background (deep red or black) to differentiate it from the main content area. Inside, the content is arranged in a single horizontal line: on the left, a concise label such as “Reading List” with a count of saved items; in the center or slightly left-weighted, a horizontally scrollable row of small article chips or thumbnails; and on the right, a prominent but compact CTA button (e.g., “View All”) styled similarly to the site’s primary CTAs but scaled down. Text is in light color (white or light gold) to stand out against the dark background. This strip remains visible as users scroll through the results, acting as a persistent, footer-like anchor that subtly pulls the eye downward once users finish scanning the main list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed to the bottom of the viewport, overlaying the main page content when the page is scrolled. Page content scrolls behind it, with sufficient bottom padding in the main layout so that the last result items are not obscured by the strip.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state occupies the same central vertical region as the article results list and appears only when no matching resources are found. It is positioned directly beneath the results summary, maintaining the same top alignment the list would have. The empty-state container is centered within the max-width grid and spans roughly 6–8 columns, leaving generous side margins to create a lighter, more open feel than the dense list view. It includes an icon or illustrative element at the top center, a bold headline immediately below that, a short explanatory text, and one or two action buttons (e.g., ‘Clear Filters’ or ‘Browse All Topics’) aligned center. Vertical padding inside the panel is generous (24–32px top and bottom), and the surrounding vertical whitespace is increased compared to the list view, so the empty state feels like a self-contained, prominent panel in the middle of the page. The reading-list strip at the bottom of the viewport still appears, ensuring continuity with the rest of the site.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in the document flow that replaces the results list in its designated area; it scrolls like normal content and does not have sticky or floating behavior.

name: Article Detail
title: Article
description: Displays the full content of a selected legal article, allows the user to save it to their reading list, and provides navigation to related articles and topic-based resources.

core_features: Load and display a single article based on the `article_id` URL parameter using the `getArticleDetail` interface, including title, content, metadata, and related articles.
Indicate whether the currently viewed article is already in the user’s reading list using `getArticleDetail.is_saved_to_reading_list`.
Allow the user to save the current article to their reading list via the `saveArticleToReadingList` interface and update the page state based on the response.
Display a list or panel of saved articles (reading list preview) by calling the `getReadingList` interface, allowing navigation to any saved article detail.
Provide navigation back to topic-filtered resources (articles listing) using links that set URL parameters for `resources.html` (`content=articles`, `topic=<article.topic>`).
Provide navigation to related articles by linking each related article entry to `article.html?article_id=<related.id>`.
user_workflows:
View an article from Resources or Home:
1. User clicks an article link on `resources.html` or `index.html` that navigates to `article.html?article_id=<selected_article_id>`.
2. On page load, the `article-loader` component reads the `article_id` URL parameter.
3. `article-loader` calls `getArticleDetail(article_id)`.
4. The page binds the returned `article` object to the header, metadata, and content components, and binds `related_articles` to the related-articles list.
5. The bookmark control reads `is_saved_to_reading_list` from the response to set its initial state (e.g., button label and disabled/active state).

Save the current article to the reading list:
1. While viewing an article, the user clicks the save/bookmark button in the bookmark control.
2. The bookmark control triggers `saveArticleToReadingList(article_id)`.
3. While waiting, the component may set an internal loading state to prevent duplicate clicks.
4. When the response returns, if `success` is true, the component updates the bookmark state to indicate that the article is saved (using the returned `reading_list.article_ids` and/or the `success` flag).
5. Optionally, if a reading list preview is open, it is refreshed by calling `getReadingList` so the newly saved article appears in the list.

Open and use the reading list preview:
1. On the article page, the user activates a control (e.g., a link or button) to open the reading list panel.
2. When the panel first opens (or on explicit refresh), the reading list component calls `getReadingList()`.
3. The component binds the returned `articles` array to a list of saved article entries.
4. The user clicks a saved article entry; the page navigates to `article.html?article_id=<saved_article_id>`.
5. The page reloads the new article via `getArticleDetail`, and the reading list panel may remain open or closed depending on design choice.

Navigate to resources filtered by topic from the article:
1. Under the article title, the user clicks a topic link or a 'Back to Articles' link.
2. The link navigates to `resources.html` and sets `content=articles` and `topic=<current_article.topic>` as URL parameters.
3. The `resources.html` page (outside this spec) uses those URL parameters to show articles filtered by that topic.

Navigate to a related article:
1. At the end or side of the article detail, the user sees a list of related articles populated from `related_articles` returned by `getArticleDetail`.
2. The user clicks a related article entry.
3. The browser navigates to `article.html?article_id=<related_article.id>`.
4. The `article-loader` component detects the new `article_id`, calls `getArticleDetail` again, and the page displays the newly selected article with updated metadata and related articles.
interactions:
Initial load / article change:
- Trigger: Page load or navigation that changes the `article_id` URL parameter.
- Behavior: `article-loader` reads `article_id` from the URL and calls `getArticleDetail(article_id)`. On success, article and related article data are bound to corresponding components; bookmark state is set from `is_saved_to_reading_list`.

Click 'Save Article' / bookmark button:
- Trigger: User clicks the bookmark/save control while viewing an article.
- Behavior: Component calls `saveArticleToReadingList(article_id)`. While pending, it may show a loading state and disable further clicks. On success, it updates its state to reflect that the article is now saved, and may update or trigger refresh of the reading list preview via `getReadingList`.

Open/close the reading list panel:
- Trigger: User clicks a control to open or close the reading list preview on the article page.
- Behavior: On first open (or if a manual refresh control is used), the reading list component calls `getReadingList()`. The component toggles between visible and hidden states without changing the current `article_id`.
- Additional interaction: User may click a refresh control in the reading list panel, which re-calls `getReadingList()`.

Click a saved article inside the reading list preview:
- Trigger: User clicks a saved article entry in the reading list panel.
- Behavior: The page navigates to `article.html?article_id=<selected_saved_article_id>`. The main article loader runs again and displays the new article.

Click topic link or 'Back to Articles':
- Trigger: User clicks a topic link displayed with the article metadata or a generic 'Back to Articles' control.
- Behavior: Browser navigates to `resources.html?content=articles&topic=<article.topic>`. No calls to the article detail interfaces occur on this page after navigation.

Click a related article:
- Trigger: User clicks an item in the related articles list.
- Behavior: Browser navigates to `article.html?article_id=<related_article.id>`, which re-triggers the article loading flow.

Error/empty-state interactions:
- Trigger: `getArticleDetail` or `getReadingList` returns an error or no matching data.
- Behavior: The corresponding components show an error or empty-state message and may display a retry control that re-calls the same interface when clicked.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver is the `article_id` URL parameter. When `article_id` changes, the page must re-load article data via `getArticleDetail` and update all bound components.

- URL Parameter: `article_id`
  - Required for the page to display article content.
  - On page load, if `article_id` exists, `getArticleDetail(article_id)` is called.
  - If `article_id` is missing or invalid, the article loader component should transition to an error state (e.g., 'Article not found') and may show a navigation link back to resources.

- Derived page state from `getArticleDetail`:
  - `article`: Drives content for header, metadata, topic link, and main content region.
  - `related_articles`: Drives the related articles list. If the array is empty, the related articles component shows an empty state.
  - `is_saved_to_reading_list`: Initializes the bookmark control state. If true, the save button may appear in a 'saved' state and may be disabled or non-clickable, following the rule that only saving (not removal) is supported by the available interfaces.

- Reading list state:
  - The reading list panel state (open/closed) is purely client-side UI state and does not affect URL parameters.
  - When opening the reading list panel for the first time (or on explicit user refresh), `getReadingList()` is called. The returned `reading_list_id` and `articles` are stored in component state and used to render entries.
  - After calling `saveArticleToReadingList`, the bookmark control may either rely on the local `success` flag and returned `reading_list.article_ids` to update its state or trigger a refresh of the `getReadingList` data if the reading list panel is open.

- Navigation to other pages:
  - To `resources.html`: Links constructed with `content=articles` and `topic=<article.topic>` where `article.topic` is read from the currently loaded `article` object.
  - To another article: Links constructed as `article.html?article_id=<target_article_id>` (from related articles list or reading list preview). Navigating to these links updates the browser location and re-triggers the article loading sequence.

components.functionality: Reads the `article_id` URL parameter, calls `getArticleDetail(article_id)`, and distributes the returned data (article, related_articles, is_saved_to_reading_list) to other components on the page.

Displays the article title and high-level metadata such as author name, publish date, topic, read time, and content type. Provides a back-to-resources/topic link that navigates with `content` and `topic` URL parameters.
Renders the full article body using the `content_html` field from the loaded `Article` entity. Ensures the content is displayed according to the HTML provided by the backend.
Displays detailed article metadata (publish date, author, topic, tags, read time, content type, featured status) and, if needed, additional legal or informational notes based on the article fields.

Shows the current save/bookmark state of the article and allows the user to save it to their reading list by calling `saveArticleToReadingList(article_id)`. Uses `is_saved_to_reading_list` from `getArticleDetail` for initial state and updates the state on successful save.
Displays a list of related articles returned by `getArticleDetail` and provides navigation links to each related article's detail page using the `article_id` parameter.
Provides an expandable panel showing the user’s current reading list. Calls `getReadingList()` to load saved articles and displays them as navigable links to their respective article detail pages.

Centralizes error and empty-state handling for API calls on the page (primarily `getArticleDetail` and `getReadingList`). Displays appropriate messages and retry controls without relying on static article data.

Coordinates the layout-level interactions among components on the article detail page, such as passing article data from `article-loader` to content components and synchronizing bookmark state with the reading list panel when necessary.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with content centered within a max-width container. Navigation uses horizontal flex-like distribution; hero content appears centered within the central portion of the grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the classic, linear top-to-bottom structure from the mockup: global header bar at the very top, a full-width navigation bar directly beneath, then a full-width article hero section with a background image. Within this hero, the article header (title and key info) is stacked vertically in the center band of the viewport, with the bookmark control positioned immediately beneath the title area as the main CTA. At the bottom edge of the hero, a narrow full-width metadata strip mirrors the original practice-areas strip. Below the hero, the article body occupies a centered content column. The related articles appear in a distinct full-width section beneath the article content. The reading-list panel appears as an overlay panel, not a permanent sidebar, preserving the vertically stacked, no-sidebar feel of the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the classic, linear top-to-bottom structure from the mockup: global header bar at the very top, a full-width navigation bar directly beneath, then a full-width article hero section with a background image. Within this hero, the article header (title and key info) is stacked vertically in the center band of the viewport, with the bookmark control positioned immediately beneath the title area as the main CTA. At the bottom edge of the hero, a narrow full-width metadata strip mirrors the original practice-areas strip. Below the hero, the article body occupies a centered content column. The related articles appear in a distinct full-width section beneath the article content. The reading-list panel appears as an overlay panel, not a permanent sidebar, preserving the vertically stacked, no-sidebar feel of the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The combined global header and navigation occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport, matching the mockup. The article hero (background image plus title and bookmark control) fills approximately 40–50% of the viewport height on initial load, allowing the courthouse/architectural image to dominate visually while keeping the article title clearly visible. The metadata strip at the hero’s bottom is a thin band about 5–8% of the viewport height. The main article content extends below and can scroll, with its first visible portion taking up the remaining initial viewport height. The related-articles section occupies a shorter band beneath the article body, similar in height to 1–2 screenfuls depending on content. Overlays like the loader and reading-list panel sit over the main content area without changing the underlying proportions.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header and navigation remain information-dense and compact, with tightly arranged text and small vertical padding. The article hero is more open: the title and bookmark control are grouped in the middle of the hero with generous vertical spacing, letting the background image breathe, similar to the homepage hero. The metadata strip is dense and single-line, echoing the compact practice-areas strip. The article body uses a readable but not overly airy density—comfortable line length, consistent paragraph spacing, and subhead spacing in the 16–24px range. Related articles are arranged compactly in a single section, with minimal but clear separation between items. Overall, the density feels professional and content-forward, not minimalist.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the same top-down Z-pattern: the eye starts at the logo in the top-left of the black header, moves to the contact/hours in the top-right, then drops to the centered navigation. From there, attention is pulled down into the article hero, where the bold white article title sits centered or center-left over the darker image. The bookmark control directly beneath the title acts as the strong CTA, similar in visual weight to the cyan hero buttons. The eye then naturally drops to the thin metadata strip at the bottom of the hero, scanning the bullet-separated metadata. Scrolling continues straight down into the article body text, and finally to the related-articles section at the bottom of the page. When active, the reading-list panel pops into the lower-right visual field, drawing attention without permanently disrupting the top-down flow.

layout.overall_description: The article detail page sits inside the same authoritative frame as the homepage: a black utility header with the firm’s logo on the top-left and contact information on the top-right, followed by a deep red full-width navigation bar with bold, all-caps menu items centered horizontally. Below this, a full-width article hero stretches across the screen, showing a darkened courthouse or architectural image as its backdrop. Centered within this hero, in a middle horizontal band that takes up roughly the central third of the hero’s height, the article title is set in large, white text and stacked over a smaller subtitle/location line. Directly beneath the title block, a prominent bookmark/save control sits as a primary CTA, styled like a solid, high-contrast button. The space above and below this central cluster is left relatively open so the background imagery provides gravitas and drama. At the very bottom of the hero, spanning the full width, a narrow, darker overlay strip displays the article’s key metadata—practice area, publication date, author, reading time—laid out in a single line with bullet separators, echoing the original practice-areas band. Below this hero band, the page transitions to a light, clean background where the main article body flows in a centered column that uses about two-thirds of the page width, with comfortable margins to the left and right. After the article text ends, a full-width related-articles section appears, using a darker, richer background and compact cards or links arranged in a single visual band, inviting further exploration. Throughout, overlays like the loader and reading-list panel appear above the main content—centered or in the lower-right—but the core experience remains a classic, vertically stacked reading flow with strong top framing and a hero-driven introduction.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-page-shell controls the overall page frame from the top of the viewport to the bottom, maintaining the classic header + navigation + hero + content stack. At the very top, it anchors a fixed-height black utility header that spans 100% of the viewport width and approximately 8–10% of the viewport height on desktop, with content constrained to a max-width central container (~1200–1440px) and 16–24px padding from the viewport edges. Directly beneath, it places the deep red navigation bar, also full-width, about 6–8% of the viewport height, with centered navigation links distributed evenly across the width. Below the nav bar, the shell defines the article hero region as a full-width section with a background image (darkened for contrast) that occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport height on initial load, with its content centered horizontally within the same max-width container and vertically aligned to the middle third of this hero band. Following the hero, the shell stacks the main article content section and the related-articles section vertically, each spanning full width but constraining textual content to the central container. Global overlays like loaders and error states are positioned within this shell so they align directly under the nav bar and above the scrollable content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static layout controller; header and nav portions may be sticky at the top on scroll, while the content beneath scrolls within the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay that covers the entire content area beneath the global navigation when data is being fetched. It spans 100% of the viewport width and extends from just under the navigation bar to the bottom of the viewport, leaving the header and nav visible to maintain orientation. The overlay uses a dark, slightly blurred background (e.g., translucent black) so that the hero and content structure are faintly visible underneath, reflecting the firm’s formal aesthetic. At the center of the visible viewport area, a loading indicator and optional short status text are centered both horizontally and vertically, matching the central focus used for hero content. The overlay respects the max-width container, with the spinner and text aligned with the same central column used by the article hero and body content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay relative to the viewport (anchored under the nav), blocking interaction with underlying content until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-header sits inside the article hero section, occupying the central band of the hero’s height. Horizontally, it is centered within the max-width container and spans roughly 8–10 of the 12 grid columns, leaving generous left and right margins so the background image frames the text. Vertically, it is positioned so that its visual center aligns with the vertical center of the hero: there is roughly 48–64px of vertical space between the top of the hero and the start of the title block, and a similar amount below the bookmark control cluster, allowing the background image to remain prominent. The main article title is set in large, bold, white or very light text, likely breaking into one or two lines, with a line-height that occupies about one-third of the hero’s visual height. Directly beneath, separated by 8–16px, a smaller, all-caps or semi-caps line displays the practice area or section (e.g., “CIVIL LITIGATION • NEW YORK”), echoing the secondary location statement from the homepage hero. The whole article-header block is left-aligned within its central column (for a more editorial, legal feel) but remains visually centered in the hero due to the surrounding margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; its position is responsive but always remains in the middle vertical band of the hero on desktop, shifting to vertically centered or slightly upper-centered on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-bookmark-control is positioned directly beneath the article-header’s subtitle/metadata line within the hero’s central column, acting as the hero’s primary CTA. It is separated from the subtitle by approximately 24–32px of vertical space, forming a distinct but connected cluster. The control appears as a single, prominent button or pill-shaped toggle with a strong accent color (e.g., cyan or gold) that stands out against the dark, muted background image and the white title text. It spans roughly the width of its label plus 24–32px horizontal padding on each side, resulting in a button about 25–35% of the content column width on desktop. The button is horizontally aligned with the title (left-aligned within the central column), but because the column is centered in the viewport, it appears roughly in the middle of the page. Additional helper text (e.g., “Save to Reading List”) can sit just below the button in a smaller, subdued style if needed, with an 8–12px gap. Overall, the bookmark control occupies a compact area but is given enough breathing room above and below to feel intentional and important, mirroring the visual pull of the dual CTAs on the homepage hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero content cluster; on smaller devices it may center horizontally within the hero column but remains directly under the title area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-metadata-panel forms a narrow horizontal band at the very bottom of the hero section, mimicking the practice-areas strip from the mockup. It spans the full browser width but constrains its content to the central max-width container aligned with the article-header. The panel’s height is relatively small—approximately 56–72px on desktop—so it feels like a subtle footer to the hero rather than a full section. Its background is a slightly darker, semi-opaque overlay atop the hero image (e.g., near-black with partial transparency) to ensure legibility while maintaining continuity with the hero. Inside, the metadata items (e.g., publication date, author name, practice area, reading time) are laid out in a single horizontal line on desktop, left-aligned within the container and separated by bullet separators or small vertical dividers. There is about 16–24px of horizontal padding on both sides of the text within the container. On narrower screens, the metadata can wrap into two lines but still respects the overall narrow band height as much as possible. Visually, this strip acts as the anchor at the bottom of the hero, signaling the transition from the dramatic hero imagery to the more neutral article body below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, docked to the bottom edge of the hero section; it scrolls away with the hero and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-content section begins immediately below the hero’s metadata strip, creating a clear visual break from the image-heavy top region. It stretches across the full viewport width but uses a light, neutral background distinct from the hero to support extended reading. Inside this section, the text is constrained to a centered content column spanning roughly 8–9 of the 12 grid columns (about 60–70% of the page width), with generous left and right margins to keep line lengths comfortable. The top edge of this section contains a vertical offset of about 48–64px from the metadata strip before the first line of body text, easing the transition into the reading experience. Paragraph spacing follows the established spacing system, with 16–24px between paragraphs and 24–32px above headings. Inline figures or callouts occupy the width of the text column, with occasional full-width callouts allowed as distinct sub-sections. The bottom of the article-content section maintains a similar 48–64px padding before the next major section (related articles), aligning with the moderate but professional content density seen in the mockup.
