component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content area; it simply extends vertically as long as the article requires within the central layout column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles-list occupies a dedicated full-width section directly beneath the article-content, serving as the page’s lower anchor similar in role (though not shape) to the practice-areas strip in the hero. This section uses a richer, darker background—such as deep red or a dark neutral—to visually separate it from the main reading area and to echo the navigation bar’s authority. Within this section, content is again constrained to the central max-width container, with a top padding of about 32–48px before any text or cards. A concise section heading (e.g., “Related Articles” or “You May Also Be Interested In”) is left-aligned near the top of the container, followed by a single row or two of related article items. On desktop, these items are arranged horizontally across the grid, typically 3–4 items per row, each card occupying 3–4 of the 12 columns, with 16–24px horizontal gaps. Each item is compact but legible, with a title, possibly a short descriptor, and a link treatment consistent with the site’s typography. Vertical padding at the bottom of the section is around 32–48px, so the section feels self-contained but not overly tall. This band sits near the bottom of the page content, encouraging further navigation after the user finishes reading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it is the last primary content section in the vertical stack and scrolls into view after the article body.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-panel appears only when invoked (for example, after the user toggles the bookmark control) and is designed as an overlay panel rather than a fixed sidebar, preserving the page’s vertically stacked layout. On desktop, the panel is anchored to the lower-right portion of the viewport, overlaying the article-content and/or related-articles sections without shifting them. It typically occupies about 30–35% of the viewport width and 50–60% of the viewport height, with its top edge positioned roughly at mid-height and its bottom edge several base units (16–24px) above the bottom of the viewport. The panel has a solid dark background (black or very deep red) with a subtle border or shadow to distinguish it from the underlying content. Inside, a compact header bar at the top of the panel (48–56px tall) displays the title “Reading List” and a close icon aligned to the top-right. Below this, the list of saved articles scrolls within the panel, with items stacked vertically and separated by 8–16px. The content aligns to the same typographic system as the rest of the site, using smaller, dense text to keep the panel efficient. On mobile, the panel behaves like a bottom sheet: it spans the full width of the viewport and slides up from the bottom, covering about 60–70% of the height and leaving the header/nav visible above. In both cases, an overlay dimming the underlying content increases focus and indicates modal behavior.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily fixed overlay panel (slide-in from the right on desktop, slide-up from the bottom on mobile), sitting above the main content and dismissible by the user.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-level-error-handler commandeers the main content area when a load or retrieval error occurs, replacing the article hero and article-content regions with a clearly visible but still formal error state. It appears directly beneath the global navigation bar, spanning the full width of the viewport. Within this area, it maintains the same central max-width container, with generous top and bottom padding (64–96px) to set the message apart. The error content is centered horizontally in the container but left-aligned in terms of text, reflecting the professional tone. A succinct error headline is followed by explanatory text and a primary action (such as a button to retry or go back to the articles list), arranged in a vertical stack. The visual treatment may use the firm’s deep red accent as a top border or icon accent, with a light but slightly tinted background for the error block to differentiate it from a normal article. The footer or related-articles section may be omitted or replaced by a minimal suggestion area, but the error handler’s main region dominates the content area where the hero and article would normally sit.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static replacement of the main content region; when active, it occupies the hero/body area under the navigation and scrolls normally if its content exceeds the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-header sits inside the article hero section, occupying the central band of the hero’s height. Horizontally, it is centered within the max-width container and spans roughly 8–10 of the 12 grid columns, leaving generous left and right margins so the background image frames the text. Vertically, it is positioned so that its visual center aligns with the vertical center of the hero: there is roughly 48–64px of vertical space between the top of the hero and the start of the title block, and a similar amount below the bookmark control cluster, allowing the background image to remain prominent. The main article title is set in large, bold, white or very light text, likely breaking into one or two lines, with a line-height that occupies about one-third of the hero’s visual height. Directly beneath, separated by 8–16px, a smaller, all-caps or semi-caps line displays the practice area or section (e.g., “CIVIL LITIGATION • NEW YORK”), echoing the secondary location statement from the homepage hero. The whole article-header block is left-aligned within its central column (for a more editorial, legal feel) but remains visually centered in the hero due to the surrounding margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; its position is responsive but always remains in the middle vertical band of the hero on desktop, shifting to vertically centered or slightly upper-centered on smaller viewports.

name: Estate Package Builder
title: Estate Package Builder
description: Interactive page where users select estate planning documents and add-ons, set their client context and budget, see a dynamically calculated package price, and proceed to a summary once requirements and budget constraints are satisfied.

core_features: Load builder configuration via getEstatePackageBuilderConfig, including available estate document templates, add-on options, supported client types, supported states, and currency.
Allow users to set or adjust client_type, state, and max_budget, with initial values optionally taken from URL parameters.
Display all available EstateDocumentTemplate items with selection controls, reflecting which are marked as required by the configuration and preventing removal of required documents if necessary.
Display all available EstateAddOnOption items with selection controls so users can add or remove optional enhancements to their estate package.

On any relevant change (client_type, state, document selection, add-on selection, max_budget), call createOrUpdateEstatePackage to calculate and display the current EstatePackage total_price, currency, and budget status.
Show a clear budget status indicator using within_budget and budget_message returned from createOrUpdateEstatePackage, highlighting when the package exceeds the user’s max_budget.
Validate that required documents are included and that, if a max_budget is provided, the package is within budget before enabling the "Continue" / "Review Package" button.

Navigate to estate_package_summary.html using URL parameters (including at minimum the estate_package.id and any relevant context such as client_type, state, and max_budget) when the user clicks "Continue" / "Review Package" and validation passes.
user_workflows: Initial load: User visits estate_package_builder.html optionally with client_type, state, and max_budget URL parameters. The page parses these parameters, then calls getEstatePackageBuilderConfig. Once configuration is loaded, the page populates client type and state selectors with the supported values, pre-fills them from URL parameters when valid, and initializes the budget field from max_budget if present. Required documents from the configuration may be auto-selected.

Configuring context: If client_type or state are not preselected or are invalid, the user chooses values from dropdowns or radio controls populated from supported_client_types and supported_states. When both client_type and state are set, the page ensures all required documents are selected and triggers the first call to createOrUpdateEstatePackage using current selections and any available max_budget.

Selecting documents: The user selects or deselects optional EstateDocumentTemplate items via checkboxes or similar controls. Required templates (as indicated by is_required) are either pre-selected and locked or clearly labeled and enforced via validation. After each change, the page calls createOrUpdateEstatePackage with the updated selected_document_ids and previously chosen client_type, state, selected_add_on_ids, and max_budget, then updates the displayed total_price and budget status.

Selecting add-ons: The user optionally selects or deselects EstateAddOnOption items via checkboxes or tier selectors. Each change triggers a call to createOrUpdateEstatePackage to refresh the EstatePackage total_price and budget information. The page shows which add-ons are currently part of the package.

Adjusting budget: The user can enter or edit max_budget in a numeric input. On change or blur (once the input is valid), the page calls createOrUpdateEstatePackage with the same client_type, state, selected_document_ids, selected_add_on_ids, and the new max_budget. The returned within_budget flag and budget_message update the budget indicator and determine whether the package is treated as within the user’s budget.

Reviewing package readiness: As the user changes inputs, the page continuously evaluates whether all required documents are included and whether the package is within budget (if max_budget is provided). When both conditions are satisfied, the "Continue" / "Review Package" button becomes enabled. If conditions are not met, the button remains disabled, and explanatory validation messages are shown.

Proceeding to summary: When the user clicks the enabled "Continue" / "Review Package" button, the page uses the most recent estate_package object returned by createOrUpdateEstatePackage to construct a navigation URL to estate_package_summary.html, passing at least estate_package.id as a URL parameter and, optionally, client_type, state, and max_budget for context. The browser navigates to this URL for the user to review the package details.
interactions: On page initialization, parse URL search parameters (client_type, state, max_budget). Then call getEstatePackageBuilderConfig. Once resolved, bind configuration data to client context selectors and selection lists, and attempt to pre-select client_type and state if they match supported values.

When the user changes the client_type selector: update internal state, optionally update the URL parameter for client_type using history APIs, ensure required documents remain selected, and if state is already selected, call createOrUpdateEstatePackage to refresh the package.

When the user changes the state selector: update internal state, optionally update the URL parameter for state, re-validate that selected documents are appropriate (if the implementation needs to adjust for state), and call createOrUpdateEstatePackage when both client_type and state are set.

When the user toggles a document selection control: update selected_document_ids, enforce that required documents remain included, then call createOrUpdateEstatePackage with updated selections and refresh display of total_price and budget indicators.
When the user toggles an add-on selection control: update selected_add_on_ids, call createOrUpdateEstatePackage, and refresh the displayed total_price, within_budget flag, and budget_message.

When the user updates the max_budget field: validate the input format, update internal max_budget and the max_budget URL parameter, then call createOrUpdateEstatePackage to recompute within_budget and budget_message using the current package contents.

When the createOrUpdateEstatePackage response arrives: store the returned estate_package object (including id, total_price, currency, selected_document_ids, selected_add_on_ids, and status), update the price summary and budget status display, and re-run validation to enable or disable the continue button.

When the user clicks the "Continue" / "Review Package" button: if validation passes (required documents present and budget criteria satisfied), construct a URL for estate_package_summary.html that includes at least the estate_package.id as a query parameter and navigate there. If validation fails, prevent navigation and show or focus relevant validation messages.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary state is derived from a combination of URL parameters, configuration from getEstatePackageBuilderConfig, and the most recent estate_package returned by createOrUpdateEstatePackage. On load, the page reads URL search parameters client_type, state, and max_budget. It does not use localStorage or other client persistence. After getEstatePackageBuilderConfig returns, the page checks whether the URL-supplied client_type and state are members of supported_client_types and supported_states. If they are not, the corresponding fields remain unset and the invalid URL parameters are ignored for functional purposes. A valid numeric max_budget from the URL is stored in the page state and used in calls to createOrUpdateEstatePackage; if missing or invalid, max_budget is treated as undefined until the user inputs a value. As the user modifies client_type, state, or max_budget, the page updates the URL parameters via pushState or replaceState so the current configuration is reflected in the URL without reloading. Internal state for selected_document_ids and selected_add_on_ids comes from user interactions and is synchronized with the estate_package data returned from createOrUpdateEstatePackage; if the response’s selected_document_ids or selected_add_on_ids differ (for example, due to server-side adjustments), the page state is updated to match the response. Validation state (whether required documents are included and whether the package is within budget) is recomputed after each successful createOrUpdateEstatePackage call, using the configuration’s is_required flags and the response’s within_budget flag. The decision to enable or disable navigation to estate_package_summary.html is based solely on this computed validation state and not cached anywhere else.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by parsing URL parameters, calling getEstatePackageBuilderConfig, and providing configuration data and initial context (client_type, state, max_budget) to other components.
Captures and manages user inputs for client_type, state, and max_budget, pre-filling them from URL parameters when valid, and triggers package recalculation when these values change.

Displays all available EstateDocumentTemplate items returned from getEstatePackageBuilderConfig, indicates which are required, allows selection or deselection of optional documents, and keeps selected_document_ids in sync with the current EstatePackage.

Displays all available EstateAddOnOption items returned from getEstatePackageBuilderConfig, grouped or labeled as appropriate (for example, by tier if tier is present), and lets users select or deselect add-ons while updating selected_add_on_ids in the current EstatePackage.

Shows the current EstatePackage total_price and currency as returned from createOrUpdateEstatePackage, displays the budget_message and within_budget status, and exposes a computed flag indicating whether the package meets both required-document and budget criteria.

Aggregates and displays validation feedback related to missing required documents, unset client_type or state, and budget issues based on within_budget and configuration data, updating whenever inputs or the EstatePackage change.

Provides the "Continue" / "Review Package" button, enabling it only when the current EstatePackage includes all required documents and satisfies the budget condition if max_budget is defined, and navigates to estate_package_summary.html with the estate_package.id and relevant context in the URL when clicked.

Centralizes calls to createOrUpdateEstatePackage whenever client_type, state, selected_document_ids, selected_add_on_ids, or max_budget change, and broadcasts the latest estate_package, within_budget, and budget_message to dependent components.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with content centered within a max-width container. Navigation uses horizontal flex-like distribution; hero content appears centered within the central portion of the grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global header, navigation, and hero shell, the Estate Package Builder occupies a single, vertically stacked, full-width content band that visually behaves like the hero's central content cluster. Inside this band, components follow a top-to-bottom sequence: page title and validation/status at the top of the work area, then the client context form, followed by the main selection lists (documents then add-ons), then a prominent price-and-budget summary band, and finally a persistent action/footer bar at the very bottom of the viewport. The data-loader and service-connector are layered utilities rather than primary visual blocks: the loader appears as a full-viewport overlay when active; the sync component appears as a small status element attached to the summary band.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global header, navigation, and hero shell, the Estate Package Builder occupies a single, vertically stacked, full-width content band that visually behaves like the hero's central content cluster. Inside this band, components follow a top-to-bottom sequence: page title and validation/status at the top of the work area, then the client context form, followed by the main selection lists (documents then add-ons), then a prominent price-and-budget summary band, and finally a persistent action/footer bar at the very bottom of the viewport. The data-loader and service-connector are layered utilities rather than primary visual blocks: the loader appears as a full-viewport overlay when active; the sync component appears as a small status element attached to the summary band.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header and navigation still occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. The Estate Package Builder work area then fills the remaining vertical space, visually echoing the hero's dominant region. Within this work area, the client context form takes the top ~20–25% of the content column height, the document and add-on lists together occupy about 45–55% (with documents slightly larger than add-ons), and the price-and-budget summary band occupies about 15–20%, depending on content depth. The sticky package actions footer is a thin bar (5–8% of viewport height) fixed to the bottom edge, similar in height to the practice-areas strip in the mockup. Horizontally, all primary components are centered within a max-width container (~70–80% of viewport width, up to around 1100–1200px), respecting the implicit 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density follows the mockup's pattern: moderately dense, professional, and information-forward rather than minimalist. Form fields, list items, and summary details are packed efficiently with 16–24px vertical spacing between field groups and list rows, and 24–32px padding inside cards or panels. Section separations (between context form, document list, add-on list, and summary) use 24–32px vertical gaps to create clear delineation without feeling airy. The sticky footer bar is compact and efficient, with tightly arranged but readable CTAs akin to the navigation style.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows a top-down Z-pattern adapted to this tool. Within the builder band, the eye starts at a left-aligned section title and any prominent validation banner near the top, moves horizontally to the right where high-level status (such as budget fit or sync status) may sit, then drops down into the central column of interactive form fields and selection lists. The strong, high-contrast price-and-budget summary band toward the lower-middle acts like the hero CTA cluster, pulling attention downward. Finally, the saturated, full-width sticky action bar at the bottom provides the ultimate anchor, similar to the practice-areas strip, drawing the eye to the primary actions once the inputs and price feel right.

layout.overall_description: Inside the law firm’s existing dark, authoritative frame, the Estate Package Builder appears as a tall, centered work surface that visually replaces the hero’s tagline-and-CTA cluster with an application-like flow. A muted courthouse-style backdrop or dark tonal field sits behind a semi-opaque central container, which spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width and is aligned to the center, respecting a 12-column grid. At the top of this container, a bold, left-aligned all-caps page title and any validation banner set the tone, with sync and high-level status details tucked toward the right. Below that, the client context form forms a structured band, followed by stacked bands for document and add-on selection that feel like dense, professional lists without wasted space. Beneath these, a wide, dark, and slightly elevated price-and-budget summary panel stretches across the content width, with the calculated price and budget alignment laid out horizontally, echoing the hero’s central CTA treatment. Anchoring the entire experience, a slim but visually strong action bar in deep red is fixed to the bottom of the viewport, spanning edge to edge with clear, high-contrast buttons aligned primarily to the right. Everything flows vertically, with consistent 24–32px gaps between sections and approximately 48px padding top and bottom within the main work area to let the content breathe while preserving the formal, information-first aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that sits above all other content, including the global header, navigation, and the builder work area. When active, it covers 100% of the width and height of the viewport with a semi-transparent black or deep charcoal scrim (around 60–70% opacity), subtly allowing the underlying courthouse-themed background and page structure to show through. At the visual center of the viewport, a compact loading card (roughly 320–400px wide) is centered both vertically and horizontally, with 24–32px internal padding and a slight drop shadow. Within this card, a small spinner or progress indicator sits above a concise, all-caps status label such as “PREPARING ESTATE PACKAGE,” aligned centrally. The container respects the 12-column grid but is visually decoupled from the central content column, emphasizing that the entire experience is in a temporary loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay covering the entire viewport; positioned above all other components via a high z-index; scroll on the underlying page is effectively disabled while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The client context form occupies the upper portion of the central builder container, immediately below the page title and any validation banner. It spans the full width of the content column (approximately 70–80% of the viewport width, constrained to around 1100–1200px max), centered horizontally on the page. Vertically, it forms a block that takes roughly 20–25% of the visible builder height on a typical desktop viewport, with 32–40px top padding from the container edge and 24–32px bottom margin before the next section. Inside the form, fields are organized in 1–3 logical rows, each row using the underlying 12-column grid to place 2–3 fields side by side (e.g., a 6/6 or 4/4/4 column split), maintaining 16–24px horizontal gaps between fields. Section labels and microcopy are left-aligned, and the overall content is moderately dense, with 16–20px vertical gaps between field groups. The form is visually framed with a subtle bottom border or tonal separation from the document-selection section below, reinforcing its role as the ‘step one’ context layer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scrollable flow; always appears as the first interactive section within the builder container under the title and validation messages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The document-selection list sits directly below the client context form, separated by a clear 24–32px vertical gap to signal a new decision area. It spans the same centered content width as the form (about 70–80% of viewport width, max 1100–1200px) and occupies a relatively tall band, roughly 25–35% of the builder’s vertical space, and more as content requires. The section begins with a left-aligned, all-caps heading (e.g., “CORE DOCUMENTS”) aligned to the same baseline as the form title above, ensuring a strong vertical rhythm. Below the heading, individual document options are displayed as stacked rows or compact cards, each extending nearly the full width of the column with 16–24px internal padding and 12–16px vertical spacing between items. Brief descriptions and price impacts sit beneath bold document names, preserving a clear, information-forward layout. The list reads as a dense but structured checklist, mirroring the mockup’s preference for content-rich panels over airy cards. A subtle bottom margin of 24–32px separates this section from the add-on list that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked within the main scroll; the list can extend beyond the initial viewport height, with the page scrolling to reveal more documents while the sticky package-actions-footer remains visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-on selection list continues the vertical stacking pattern and appears immediately beneath the document-selection list, with a 24–32px gap between them. It shares the same centered width and alignment, creating a unified column of decision-making content. This section is slightly more compact vertically than the document list, typically occupying about 15–25% of the builder’s vertical space depending on the number of add-ons, but it can grow as needed. A section heading (e.g., “OPTIONAL ADD-ONS”) is left-aligned at the top of the band, with add-ons listed below as shorter rows or pill-like cards. Each item aligns with the 12-column grid, with labels and brief descriptions on the left and any price delta or toggles aligned to the right edge of the content column, reinforcing a horizontal scan similar to navigation items in the mockup. Vertical spacing between add-on items is tighter (around 12–16px) than the spacing between major sections, supporting the slightly denser, secondary nature of this list. A more generous 32–40px margin separates the bottom of this section from the price-and-budget-summary panel to emphasize the transition from selection to evaluation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable within the main flow; naturally pushes the price-and-budget summary band downward as more add-ons are introduced.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price-and-budget summary is a visually prominent band that anchors the lower-middle of the builder, analogous to the hero’s central CTA cluster. It is positioned directly below the add-on selection list, starting after a 32–40px vertical gap, and spans the full width of the content column while being centered relative to the viewport. This panel has a distinct background color—likely a deep charcoal or dark red variant—with 24–32px internal padding on all sides and slightly rounded corners or a subtle border to elevate it above the page background. Vertically, it occupies about 15–20% of the builder’s height in a typical configuration, but can expand to accommodate additional summary rows. Inside, the content follows a clear left-to-right structure: on the left half (approximately 6 of 12 columns), a large, bold total package price is displayed, possibly with a smaller label above (e.g., “ESTIMATED PACKAGE TOTAL”) and any explanatory notes below. On the right half (the remaining 6 columns), budget-related information appears—such as client budget, variance, and a concise status (e.g., “WITHIN BUDGET”)—arranged in 2–3 stacked lines. This mirrors the mockup’s top-right utility cluster and reinforces the Z-pattern by drawing the eye from left price to right budget status. A small sync status indicator (estate-package-sync) is visually anchored in the upper-right corner of this panel. A 24–32px margin beneath the panel leads into the visual breathing space above the sticky footer bar at the bottom of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable content but visually behaves like an anchor near the lower-middle of the page; remains above the sticky bottom action bar even when content scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation messages panel sits at the very top of the builder’s central content column, directly below the page-level title and above the client context form. It spans 100% of the content column’s width and is horizontally centered with the rest of the builder. When present, it forms a low-profile yet noticeable horizontal band, roughly 48–72px tall depending on message content, with 16–24px internal vertical padding. The background is a high-contrast color tied to the legal brand—typically a dark red or gold accent for warnings/errors—mirroring the boldness of the navigation bar in the mockup. Text is left-aligned for quick scanning, with key phrases (e.g., “MISSING REQUIRED DOCUMENTS”) in uppercase or semi-bold to match the authoritative, all-caps navigation style. If multiple messages exist, they can be stacked within this band with 4–8px spacing between lines, maintaining a compact height. A 16–24px gap below this panel leads into the client-context-form, ensuring the messages feel attached to the top of the form but not fused with it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inserted in the flow between the page title and the client context form; when not needed, the space collapses so the form rises directly under the title.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package actions footer is a slim, full-width bar fixed to the bottom edge of the viewport, echoing the narrow practice-areas strip in the original hero but repurposed for interactive CTAs. It spans 100% of the page width from left to right and has a height of roughly 56–72px (about 5–8% of viewport height), with 16–24px vertical padding inside. The bar uses a saturated, brand-appropriate deep red or dark accent color that contrasts strongly against the page background, reinforcing its importance. Within this bar, content is aligned to the same central max-width container used above: a centered content region (70–80% of viewport width, max ~1100–1200px) holds the text and buttons. On the left side of this region, a concise status summary (e.g., “3 DOCUMENTS SELECTED · WITHIN BUDGET”) is left-aligned, using uppercase or semi-bold styling reminiscent of navigation labels. On the right side, a horizontal row of primary and secondary buttons is right-aligned, with 24–32px horizontal spacing between buttons and 12–16px internal button padding, mirroring the mockup’s CTA treatment. This bar remains fixed as the user scrolls the builder content behind it, always providing quick access to key actions like saving or proceeding to summary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed to the bottom of the viewport, independent of scroll; underlying page content scrolls beneath it, with sufficient bottom padding in the main content column so that document and summary content does not disappear behind the bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estate package sync component is a compact status element integrated into the upper-right corner of the price-and-budget-summary panel, aligning with the mockup’s use of utility details in the top-right of dense bands. Visually, it appears as a small horizontal pill or icon+label cluster, occupying roughly 2–3 columns of the 12-column grid on the far right of the summary band. It sits flush with the right padding of the panel and about 8–12px below the panel’s top edge, so it feels connected yet subordinate to the main price label. The background of the pill may be slightly lighter or darker than the panel’s base color to provide contrast without stealing focus, and the text (e.g., “SYNCED” or “SYNCING…”) is in a sharp, uppercase style with a small cloud or link icon to the left. This placement allows users to quickly verify the package’s sync state without disrupting the vertical hierarchy of context form, lists, and price summary. When additional sync details are needed (e.g., last updated), they appear as a short, secondary line beneath the main label while remaining within the same top-right corner of the panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the price-and-budget-summary component’s bounds; does not scroll separately or fix to the viewport. Its presence does not alter the panel’s overall footprint significantly, maintaining the integrity of the summary band’s layout.

name: Estate Package Summary
title: Estate Package Summary
description: Review the configured estate planning package, adjust included add-ons, confirm it meets any budget and document requirements, and save or submit the quote without payment.

core_features: Load and display the current estate package summary (client_type, state, documents, add-ons, total_price, status, label) using getEstatePackageSummary.
Show a structured list of included estate planning documents with their details for verification.
Provide an editable list of selected add-ons where users can remove or re-add add-ons within the current session and see an updated calculated total for the package.
Display a price summary section that reflects the current total from the API and any user-adjusted total based on add-on changes.
Check the package against optional budget constraints passed via URL parameters and indicate whether the package is within budget.
Validate that any required documents (passed via URL parameters as document codes) are present in the package, and show warnings if they are missing.
Allow the user to optionally label the estate package quote and save it (status saved) or proceed without payment but mark it as submitted (status submitted) using finalizeEstatePackageQuote.
Provide navigation controls to edit the package in the Estate Package Builder, start a new package, or proceed to a contact form for an attorney review after a quote is saved or submitted.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Initial load and review
1. User lands on estate_package_summary.html with a URL that includes at least an estate_package_id parameter (for routing/identification) and optionally budget and required document parameters.
2. The page initialization triggers getEstatePackageSummary, which returns estate_package, documents, and add_ons.
3. The page displays client_type and state, the list of documents, the current add-ons, and the total_price from the returned estate_package.
4. The page computes any derived state needed for display (such as a working total that can be updated when add-ons are toggled) based solely on the returned data.
5. The user reviews all sections to ensure the configuration is correct.

Workflow 2: Adjust add-ons and preview updated total
1. From the add-on list, the user clicks a control (e.g., a remove/toggle button) next to an add-on.
2. The page updates a local in-page representation of the selected_add_on_ids (a working copy) and recalculates a working total by adjusting the total_price using add_ons pricing from getEstatePackageSummary.
3. The price summary updates to show the recalculated working total separately from, or as an updated version of, the original total, without introducing any static values.
4. The user can reverse the change (e.g., re-select a previously removed add-on), and the working total is recalculated again.
5. When satisfied, the user proceeds to finalize the quote; the working configuration is treated as the intended state for submission.

Workflow 3: Budget and document requirement validation
1. The user arrives with optional URL parameters, such as a max_budget value and a required_docs list (a comma-separated list of document template codes).
2. After getEstatePackageSummary resolves, the page checks:
   - Whether the current working total (initially the API total, then adjusted if the user changes add-ons) is less than or equal to the max_budget value, if provided.
   - Whether all required_docs codes are present in the returned documents array.
3. If the package exceeds the budget, a budget status component displays a clear warning and may prevent submission until the user acknowledges or adjusts add-ons.
4. If required documents are missing, a requirements status component displays an error message, and the finalize controls are disabled or require the user to go back to Edit Package.
5. As the user modifies add-ons (and the total), the budget status is recalculated in real time based on the dynamic values returned from the API.

Workflow 4: Save or submit the package quote
1. The user enters an optional textual label for the package quote (for example, to identify it later) in a label input field.
2. The user clicks one of the primary actions:
   - A button to save the quote (persisting it without marking it as submitted), or
   - A button to proceed without payment but mark the quote as submitted.
3. The relevant button triggers a call to finalizeEstatePackageQuote with parameters:
   - label: the user-entered label value.
   - submit: a boolean derived from which button was clicked (false for save, true for proceed/submit).
4. The page awaits the response from finalizeEstatePackageQuote, which returns an updated estate_package, success, and message.
5. If success is true, the page updates the displayed estate_package status and label from the response, and shows the message in a confirmation component.
6. If success is false, an error state is shown, and no navigation occurs; the user can correct issues and retry.

Workflow 5: Request attorney review after quote saved/submitted
1. After a successful finalizeEstatePackageQuote call, the page reveals or activates a "Request Attorney Review" navigation control.
2. When clicked, this control navigates the user to contact.html with URL parameters:
   - type_of_issue=estate_planning_question (constant value as per specification).
   - state set to the estate_package.state returned from the latest API call.
3. The user can then complete the contact form on contact.html, which will use the passed type_of_issue and state to prefill or guide the inquiry.

Workflow 6: Edit package or start over
1. From the summary page, the user can click an "Edit Package" control.
2. This interaction navigates to estate_package_builder.html with URL parameters such as:
   - estate_package_id set to estate_package.id from getEstatePackageSummary.
   - mode set to a value like edit (for the builder’s internal logic) if that parameter is used by the builder.
   - return_to=estate_package_summary.html so the builder can send the user back to this page after editing.
3. Alternatively, the user may click a "Start Over" control.
4. This navigates to estate_package_builder.html with parameters indicating a new package flow (for example, mode=new), without relying on any static or hardcoded content.
5. After changes are made in the builder and the user returns to the summary page, getEstatePackageSummary runs again to retrieve the updated configuration.
interactions:
On page load (initialization):
- Trigger getEstatePackageSummary with no parameters, relying on server-side context or routing to identify the current estate package.
- When the promise resolves, bind the returned estate_package, documents, and add_ons data to the relevant components.
- Initialize a working state object that mirrors estate_package and selected add-ons for in-page updates.

Click on add-on toggle/remove control:
- Update the working list of selected add-ons in page state.
- Recalculate the working total using the prices returned from the API for add_ons and any necessary document pricing.
- Re-run budget and document validations that depend on the total or selection.
- Rerender the add-on list and price summary components to reflect the updated working state.

Click "Edit Package":
- Construct a URL to estate_package_builder.html that includes estate_package.id and any additional context parameters (e.g., mode and return_to).
- Redirect the browser to the constructed URL.

Click "Start Over":
- Construct a URL to estate_package_builder.html with parameters that indicate a new configuration flow.
- Redirect the browser to that URL.

Change label input value:
- Update the in-page label state used when calling finalizeEstatePackageQuote.
- Optionally validate that the label is within any length or character constraints before submission.

Click "Save Package Quote":
- Validate that all required documents (from URL parameters) are present and that budget constraints are satisfied or appropriately acknowledged.
- Call finalizeEstatePackageQuote with submit=false and label equal to the current label state value.
- On success, update displayed estate_package.status and estate_package.label with values from the response and show the returned message.
- On error, show an error message and allow the user to retry.

Click "Proceed Without Payment":
- Perform the same validations as "Save Package Quote".
- Call finalizeEstatePackageQuote with submit=true and label equal to the current label state value.
- On success, update displayed estate_package.status and estate_package.label from the response and display the message.
- Offer navigation to contact.html for attorney review after success.

Click "Request Attorney Review":
- Use the most recent estate_package.state from the last successful API response.
- Redirect to contact.html with URL parameters type_of_issue=estate_planning_question and state set to the estate_package.state value.

Budget status acknowledgement (if applicable):
- If the package exceeds max_budget, present an interaction (such as a checkbox) where the user acknowledges proceeding over budget.
- Only enable finalize buttons after acknowledgment or after the user adjusts add-ons to meet the budget.

Handling API failures for getEstatePackageSummary:
- If the initial summary fetch fails, show a functional error message and provide a navigation control back to estate_package_builder.html without relying on any hardcoded data content.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL parameters for all navigable state and contextual constraints, and uses in-memory state only for transient interactions:

- URL Parameters consumed by estate_package_summary.html:
  - estate_package_id: Identifies which estate package is being summarized. While getEstatePackageSummary itself takes no parameters, this URL parameter informs the server-side context (e.g., via routing or session mapping) about which EstatePackage instance to expose. If this parameter is missing or invalid, the page displays an error and offers navigation to estate_package_builder.html for the user to start or select a package.
  - max_budget: An optional numeric parameter indicating the user's budget ceiling for the package. After getEstatePackageSummary loads, the page compares the working total against this value. When the user adjusts add-ons, the working total is recomputed and compared again. This parameter is never hardcoded on the client; it is always read from the URL.
  - required_docs: An optional comma-separated list of EstateDocumentTemplate codes that must be included in the package. Once documents are loaded from the summary API, the page parses required_docs from the URL, checks for the presence of each code within documents, and updates a requirements status indicator. If required_docs is absent, all document requirements are considered satisfied.
  - mode: An optional mode value (such as review or readonly) read from the URL and used to determine whether editing controls (such as add-on toggles) should be interactive. For example, if mode=readonly, the add-on editing controls and finalize buttons can be disabled.
  - return_to: An optional parameter specifying the URL to return to after navigating to other pages (primarily used by the builder). The summary page reads this only for consistency when generating navigation links, and ensures that any constructed URLs encode it properly.

- In-page (transient) state:
  - workingEstatePackage: A client-side object initialized from the estate_package values returned by getEstatePackageSummary. This object holds the current working representation of the package as the user toggles add-ons; it does not introduce any hardcoded values.
  - workingAddOnsSelection: A representation of which add-ons are currently selected in the summary UI, initialized from the API's add_ons list (and any known selection flags). Toggling an add-on updates this state.
  - workingTotalPrice: A numeric field derived from estate_package.total_price and dynamically updated when the user toggles add-ons, using the add_ons price data returned from the API. The calculation is always based on the API-supplied data and is not hardcoded.
  - labelInput: The current value of the label input field. This is passed to finalizeEstatePackageQuote as the label parameter.
  - budgetStatus: A derived state (e.g., withinBudget, overBudget) computed by comparing workingTotalPrice to max_budget from the URL when that parameter is present.
  - requirementsStatus: A derived state (e.g., allRequiredDocsPresent, missingRequiredDocs) computed by comparing required_docs from the URL to the codes in documents.

- Navigation state (via URLs):
  - Navigation to estate_package_builder.html includes at least estate_package_id and may include mode and return_to so that the builder can restore or modify the package and then return the user to this summary page.
  - Navigation to contact.html passes type_of_issue=estate_planning_question and state equal to estate_package.state, ensuring the contact page is context-aware.

- Persistence and interfaces:
  - Only getEstatePackageSummary and finalizeEstatePackageQuote are invoked from this page. No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used. Any persisted changes to the package occur only when finalizeEstatePackageQuote is called, with the backend responsible for interpreting the current session state and the label and submit parameters.
  - All dynamic values shown on the page are directly derived from API responses or URL parameters, ensuring no conflicting static or hardcoded data.

components.functionality: On page initialization, fetches the current estate package summary using getEstatePackageSummary, initializes working in-page state (workingEstatePackage, workingAddOnsSelection, workingTotalPrice), and triggers rendering of dependent components.
Displays top-level information about the estate package, including client_type, state, status, label, and timestamps, based on the current workingEstatePackage state.
Shows the list of included estate planning documents with their names and optional descriptions from the documents array so the user can verify that required documents are included.
Evaluates whether all documents specified in the required_docs URL parameter are present in the documents list and indicates if any are missing, potentially disabling finalize actions until resolved.

Displays all selected add-ons from the add_ons array and allows the user to toggle each add-on on or off in the workingAddOnsSelection state. When toggled, it recalculates workingTotalPrice using prices from the API and updates the price summary and budget status.

Shows the current package price, including the original total_price from estate_package and the workingTotalPrice that reflects any in-page add-on changes. It updates automatically whenever workingTotalPrice changes.

Reads max_budget from the URL, compares it to workingTotalPrice, and indicates whether the package is within or over budget. It can gate finalize actions (e.g., requiring acknowledgment or preventing submission) based on this comparison.
Provides an input field where the user can set or update the label for the estate package quote. The value is stored in labelInput state and passed to finalizeEstatePackageQuote.

Hosts the primary actions to save the package quote or proceed without payment. It validates budget and document requirements before calling finalizeEstatePackageQuote, and then updates the estate package state and displays messages based on the API response.
Displays success or error messages returned from finalizeEstatePackageQuote, along with the updated status of the estate package.

Provides navigation controls for editing the package in the builder, starting a new package, and requesting attorney review after a successful quote finalization. It constructs destination URLs using estate_package.id, estate_package.state, and URL parameters like return_to.

Handles and displays error states related to data fetching (getEstatePackageSummary) or finalization (finalizeEstatePackageQuote) and ensures the user always has a way to navigate back to the builder.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with content centered within a max-width container. Navigation uses horizontal flex-like distribution; hero content appears centered within the central portion of the grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Inside the existing site shell (black utility header + red nav), the Estate Package Summary page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow of full-width sections within a centered max-width container. Immediately below the global nav, a prominent "summary band" functions like a sub-hero: it presents the package header, status indicators, and price summary, with the quote label form integrated into this top region. Below that, a main configuration section holds the document list and add-on editor in a structured layout, followed by a lower decision band containing finalization controls and feedback. A thin navigation-actions strip at the very bottom echoes the narrow practice-areas strip from the hero in the mockup. Loader and error components overlay or sit above this flow when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Inside the existing site shell (black utility header + red nav), the Estate Package Summary page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow of full-width sections within a centered max-width container. Immediately below the global nav, a prominent "summary band" functions like a sub-hero: it presents the package header, status indicators, and price summary, with the quote label form integrated into this top region. Below that, a main configuration section holds the document list and add-on editor in a structured layout, followed by a lower decision band containing finalization controls and feedback. A thin navigation-actions strip at the very bottom echoes the narrow practice-areas strip from the hero in the mockup. Loader and error components overlay or sit above this flow when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header and nav retain roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. Below them, the in-page summary band occupies about 20–25% of the viewport height, giving the package context, statuses, and price strong visual presence. The main configuration area (documents + add-ons) uses roughly 40–50% of the viewport, forming the central working zone. The finalization controls and feedback occupy a compact band (about 10–15% of the viewport when combined), and the navigation-actions strip sits as a slim anchor (5–8% of the viewport or less). Horizontally, all core content is constrained within a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) on a 12-column grid, with key functional components (documents and add-ons) sharing the width in a 7/12–5/12 proportion.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page preserves the mockup’s moderately dense, professional feel. The summary band uses larger type, stronger contrast, and more vertical padding (~32–48px) to feel authoritative while still compact. The document list and add-on editor are denser, with tighter vertical spacing (~8–16px between items) to support scanning and editing without excessive scrolling. Status indicators and feedback messages are concise badges or bars, keeping their height minimal. The finalization controls form a clearly delineated band with medium padding (~16–24px) so actions feel important but not oversized or airy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a disciplined top-down Z-pattern. The eye moves from any global errors just below the nav to the left-aligned package header, then across to the right-aligned status indicators and price summary within the top band. From there, attention drops into the central configuration area, first to the larger, left-hand document list, then scanning rightwards to the add-on editor. As the user scrolls down, the strong horizontal band of finalization controls and the color-coded finalization feedback pull focus toward the bottom decision area. Finally, the thin navigation-actions strip at the very bottom acts like the mockup’s practice-areas strip, providing a subtle horizontal terminus for the page.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s dark header and bold red navigation, the main content opens with a wide, authoritative summary band that feels like a sub-hero: a deep, muted background spans edge-to-edge within the content container, with the estate package name and client information set in strong, left-aligned typography. On the right side of this band, compact status badges and a boxed price summary anchor the eye, echoing the mockup’s emphasis on top-right critical information. Just beneath the header text, a discreet inline form allows naming the quote, keeping configuration context and labeling tightly connected. Scrolling down, the layout transitions into a working zone where the document list dominates the left two-thirds of the grid and the add-on editor occupies the right third, mirroring the central weight of the hero content in the mockup but expressed as functional lists rather than imagery. Each of these sections is framed with subtle borders and consistent padding, creating clear but dense work areas. Near the bottom, a full-width decision band with finalization controls and clear CTAs stretches across the container, with feedback messages immediately above it to confirm save/submit outcomes. Finally, a narrow, understated navigation-actions strip runs along the bottom of the content, acting like a footer-like guide rail that gently concludes the vertical flow, much like the practice-area strip in the original hero. Throughout, overlays for loading and errors temporarily sit atop this structure, dimming or displacing content while maintaining the same centered, formal, and high-contrast visual language.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary-loader appears only when data is being fetched. It sits above all in-page components but below the global header and nav, covering the content area from just under the red navigation bar down to the bottom of the viewport. It behaves as a full-width, full-height scrim within the main content region, using a semi-transparent black overlay that dims the estate summary, lists, and controls beneath. At the visual center of this overlay (both horizontally and vertically within the viewport), a compact loading indicator and short status message are displayed, occupying roughly 20–30% of the content width and about 10% of its height. All underlying components remain in their positions but are visually subdued and non-interactive while the loader is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed-position overlay within the content frame, spanning 100% of the content width and height from below the global nav to the viewport bottom; it blocks interaction with underlying components while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-header is the first in-page component, placed directly below any error message bar and the global navigation, at the top of the main content container. It sits inside a full-width, horizontally oriented summary band that spans the entire max-width container (approximately 80–90% of the viewport width on desktop) and uses generous vertical padding (~32–48px top and bottom). Within this band, the left 60–65% of the width is dedicated to the package-header: the primary package title appears near the top-left of this region in large, authoritative typography, followed by secondary lines (client name, reference number) stacked vertically beneath with 8–16px spacing. The header text is left-aligned and anchored roughly one base unit (16–24px) from the inner left edge of the container, mirroring the logo positioning in the mockup. To the right, space is intentionally reserved for the status indicators and price summary so that the entire band reads as a cohesive top summary area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the page’s main content area, forming the top section of a visually distinct summary band that does not scroll independently from the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The document-list is located in the central configuration region, directly below the summary band that houses the package-header and related elements. It occupies the left primary column of the grid, spanning approximately 7 of 12 columns (about 58–65% of the content width). A section heading for documents aligns vertically under the left edge of the package-header text, preserving a strong left-line alignment across sections. Below the heading, the list items are stacked vertically with moderate spacing (8–16px between rows), creating a dense yet legible list. The document-list extends downward and can grow taller than the viewport; if so, the page scrolls normally rather than confining scroll to the list. Horizontal padding inside the list card is about 16–24px, and there is a consistent gap (~24–32px) between the bottom of the summary band and the top of the document-list, echoing the spacing between nav and hero in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element anchored in the left portion of the main configuration section; it scrolls with the page and shares a horizontal row with the add-on-list-editor on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The requirements-status-indicator appears within the top summary band, on the right side but still visually connected to the package-header. It sits in the upper-right quadrant of the band, horizontally aligned with the baseline of the primary package title or just below it. The indicator is rendered as a compact pill or badge, approximately 10–15% of the band’s width, with a high-contrast background (e.g., deep green for "met" or deep red for "unmet") and all-caps or small-caps label text, reflecting the bold, authoritative style of the mockup's navigation. It is positioned to the left of or slightly above the budget-status-indicator, with a horizontal gap of roughly 8–12px between them, forming a tight cluster of status signals in the top-right area of the summary band. This placement ensures the user can quickly scan from the package title on the left to the compliance status on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-sticky label within the summary band; positioned using right-side alignment but moves up/down with the band as the page is scrolled.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-on-list-editor is placed to the right of the document-list within the same horizontal band of the main configuration section. On desktop, it spans approximately 5 of 12 columns (about 35–42% of the content width), starting at the grid line that aligns vertically with the right edge of the summary-band’s price-summary card. Its top edge aligns roughly with the top of the document-list heading so the two form a cohesive visual row. The add-on editor content is contained within a card-like panel with a subtle border or shadow, with internal padding of about 16–24px. List items or checkboxes are stacked vertically with relatively tight spacing (8–12px), reinforcing the dense, professional feel. When the content exceeds available vertical space, the page scrolls as a whole, but the add-on panel itself may have an internal max height that allows it to appear visually balanced with the document-list before extending further down.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element occupying the right column of the main configuration row; it scrolls with the page and adjusts its height based on content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price-summary resides in the top summary band, aligned to the far right, serving as a focal counterweight to the package-header on the left. It appears as a vertically oriented card or boxed area occupying roughly 20–30% of the band’s width and about 60–70% of its height, nested within the band’s right margin. The card is right-aligned, with its right edge approximately 16–24px from the container edge, echoing the tight right alignment of phone numbers in the mockup. The total price is displayed near the top of this card in bold, high-contrast text, with any breakdown (e.g., fees, discounts) stacked below in smaller type. There is a clear vertical separation (~8–16px) between the status badges above (requirements and budget) and the price summary card below, so the statuses appear to sit over or beside the card rather than being part of its content. This arrangement makes the financial context highly visible without overshadowing the package title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned card element within the summary band; it does not stick to the viewport but remains part of the band as it scrolls out of view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-status-indicator is paired visually with the requirements-status-indicator in the top summary band, forming a small cluster of badges in the upper-right portion of the band. It typically sits directly to the right of the requirements indicator, with a horizontal gap of about 8–12px, or directly beneath it with a vertical gap of similar size if horizontal space is constrained. Like the requirements badge, it is a small pill or label with a solid background color reflecting budget state (e.g., gold for "within budget" or deep red for "over budget"), using concise, uppercase text. This pairing allows a quick left-to-right scan of compliance and budget status just above or adjacent to the price-summary card, reinforcing the mockup’s pattern of important information anchored to the top-right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline badge in the summary band; positioned relative to the requirements-status-indicator and price-summary, moving normally as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-label-form is nestled within the lower portion of the top summary band, just beneath the package-header text on the left side. It spans roughly the left half of the container width (~50–60%), aligning its left edge with the package title and maintaining a vertical gap of about 8–16px above it. The form is compact: a single-line text input (or small set of fields) with its label either above or to the left, using modest font size to avoid competing with the header typography. The input stretches horizontally but stops before encroaching into the right-hand area reserved for the status indicators and price-summary, leaving a clear vertical corridor between them. This placement keeps quote naming intimately tied to the package identity while preserving the balanced left-right composition of the summary band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline form within the summary band; it expands vertically only as needed for its fields and scrolls out of view along with the band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-finalization-controls occupy a dedicated horizontal band near the bottom of the page content, just below the main configuration area (documents and add-ons) and immediately beneath any finalization feedback messages. This band spans the full width of the content container and has medium vertical padding (~16–24px) and a subtly distinct background (e.g., a darker neutral or muted red tone) to separate it from the lists above, similar in weight to the mockup’s CTA band within the hero. Inside this band, the left half is used for explanatory text or a brief status summary (e.g., "Review complete – choose how to proceed"), while the right half aligns one or more primary buttons (e.g., Save Quote, Submit Quote) horizontally from right to left, with button padding reflecting the design DNA (24–32px horizontal, 12–16px vertical). The buttons are visually prominent, with the primary action using the most saturated accent color. The band is tall enough to feel important but compact enough not to dominate the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal control band; it sits above the navigation-actions strip and scrolls into and out of view with the page, though it may appear close to the viewport bottom on most typical screen heights.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The finalization-feedback component appears directly above the quote-finalization-controls band, occupying the full width of the content container. It presents as a slim yet clearly visible alert bar, with height around 48–64px depending on content. Its background color changes according to state (e.g., deep green for success, gold for warning, deep red for failure), and text is centered vertically and left-aligned within the bar, starting about 16–24px from the left edge. When visible, it introduces a small vertical separation (8–16px) between itself and the control band and slightly compresses the visual distance to the content above, ensuring it is perceived as directly related to the recent finalization action. It does not occupy an excessive amount of space, maintaining the moderately dense feel while still being unmissable after action triggers.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, contextual bar that appears and disappears in the document flow; it pushes the quote-finalization-controls down when visible and scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-actions component sits at the very bottom of the main content area, forming a thin, full-width strip that visually echoes the narrow practice-areas bar at the bottom of the hero in the mockup. It spans the entire width of the content container with modest vertical padding (~8–12px) and a slightly darker background or a top border to distinguish it from the controls band above. Inside this strip, secondary navigation links (such as "Back to previous step" or "Return to quote list") are laid out horizontally: a primary back link is left-aligned near the inner left edge, while other auxiliary links (if any) are right-aligned, creating a subtle left-right tension similar to footer navigation. Text is smaller and more understated than in primary CTAs, emphasizing its tertiary, supportive nature. On shorter pages, this strip will be visible near the bottom of the viewport, while on longer pages it is reached as the user scrolls to the end.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static bottom-of-content strip; it does not stick to the viewport but naturally concludes the page’s vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-state-handler presents as a high-contrast alert bar positioned at the very top of the main content area, directly beneath the global navigation bar and above the package-header summary band. It spans the full width of the content container and has a height of about 56–72px, depending on message length. The background is a strong, deep red consistent with the site’s palette, with white or near-white text for maximum readability. The message is left-aligned, starting about 16–24px from the left edge, and may include a small icon on the far left. When active, it pushes all other content downward, preserving the linear vertical flow; when multiple errors exist, they are either consolidated into this single bar or stacked inside it rather than creating multiple bars. Its placement ensures that as users land or submit actions, their eye is drawn from the nav directly into any critical error before proceeding to the summary band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static alert bar inserted into the top of the page’s content flow; it is not sticky but will be immediately visible after a scroll-to-top behavior when triggered by critical actions.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page where potential clients submit a structured legal inquiry, with fields and options loaded dynamically and prefilled from URL parameters when available.

core_features: Load selectable options for issue type, user role, preferred contact method, and state using the getContactFormOptions interface.
Display a structured contact form bound to the ContactInquiry data model, capturing type_of_issue, user_role, amount_in_dispute, state, full_name, email, phone, message, and preferred_contact_method.
Prefill contact form fields from incoming URL parameters (type_of_issue, user_role, amount_in_dispute, state) when they match available options returned by getContactFormOptions.
Validate required fields before submission, preventing submission until required ContactInquiry fields are complete and consistent with the loaded options.
Submit the completed inquiry via the submitContactInquiry interface and handle the response, including success and failure states.
Display a confirmation area that shows the confirmation_message and key details from the created ContactInquiry after a successful submission.
Optionally display alternative contact information (such as office contact details) using Office data supplied to the page, without additional API calls from this page.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Direct access without prefilled parameters:
1) User navigates to contact.html via header or footer navigation.
2) On page load, the page controller calls getContactFormOptions to retrieve selectable options and stores them in page state.
3) Once options are loaded, the contact form becomes interactive with empty default selections.
4) User selects an issue type and user role from dropdowns populated by getContactFormOptions.
5) User optionally enters an amount in dispute and selects a state from the dynamically loaded list.
6) User enters full_name, email, optionally phone, writes a message, and chooses a preferred_contact_method from the options provided.
7) User submits the form, triggering client-side validation.
8) If validation passes, the form component calls submitContactInquiry with the current field values.
9) On success, the page displays the confirmation panel with the confirmation_message and the returned contact_inquiry data; the form can be disabled or hidden.
10) User may navigate away using global navigation or close the page.

Workflow 2 – Prefilled from another page via URL parameters:
1) User clicks a call-to-action on another page (for example, a service detail page) that links to contact.html with one or more URL parameters (type_of_issue, user_role, amount_in_dispute, state).
2) On load, the contact page controller reads the URL parameters but does not yet set form values until getContactFormOptions has completed.
3) The controller calls getContactFormOptions and receives permitted values for issue_types, user_roles, preferred_contact_methods, and states.
4) The controller compares the URL parameter values to the returned option lists:
   - If a parameter value exactly matches one of the option values, the corresponding field in the form is preselected.
   - If a parameter value does not match any available option, that field remains unselected and no error is shown.
   - For amount_in_dispute, the numeric parameter is used to prefill the numeric field if it can be parsed; otherwise, the field remains empty.
5) User reviews and can change any prefilled value before submission.
6) Submission proceeds as in Workflow 1, calling submitContactInquiry and showing the confirmation on success.

Workflow 3 – Handling validation errors and API failures:
1) User completes some, but not all, required fields and clicks submit.
2) The form component runs client-side validation:
   - If required fields are missing or invalid (including inconsistent selections that do not match the loaded options), the form shows inline validation errors and prevents the submitContactInquiry call.
3) User corrects the indicated fields and resubmits.
4) If the submitContactInquiry call fails (for example, network error or server-side validation failure communicated in the response), the form component displays a non-blocking error state and keeps the user inputs intact.
5) User can retry submission; on successful retry, the confirmation panel is shown and the error state is cleared.

Workflow 4 – Viewing alternative contact information:
1) User scrolls past or alongside the form to an optional alternative contact panel.
2) The panel displays alternative firm contact data (for example, office-level contact info) based on Office data made available to the page without additional API calls from this page.
3) User may choose to call a phone number, send an email, or navigate to an office detail URL using the links in this panel.
4) This workflow is independent of the structured form; the user may still submit the form or rely solely on direct contact methods.
interactions: On page load, the page controller automatically calls getContactFormOptions; while loading, form inputs relying on these options are disabled or in a loading state.
Selecting an issue type uses the options returned in issue_types; a change event updates the in-memory representation of the pending ContactInquiry and clears any related validation errors.
Selecting a user role similarly updates ContactInquiry state and may clear validation errors related to that field.
Changing the amount_in_dispute updates the numeric field in the pending ContactInquiry; non-numeric input is either prevented or marked invalid during validation.
Selecting a state uses the states array returned by getContactFormOptions; when the user changes this value, the page state updates and URL may optionally be synchronized with the new state value.
Entering or changing full_name, email, phone, and message updates the corresponding fields in the pending ContactInquiry model and resets any field-level validation errors.
Selecting a preferred_contact_method is done via options from preferred_contact_methods; the selected value is tracked as part of the pending ContactInquiry and must be present before submission.
Clicking the submit button triggers form validation; if valid, it initiates a call to submitContactInquiry and temporarily disables the submit button to prevent duplicate submissions.

On successful response from submitContactInquiry, the page displays a confirmation component populated with the returned contact_inquiry and confirmation_message, and may disable further edits to the submitted form instance.
If submitContactInquiry returns an error indication or the request fails, the page displays an error state while keeping user-entered data intact, allowing corrections and resubmission.

In the alternative contact panel, user interactions such as clicking a phone link, email link, or office link trigger their respective browser behaviors (e.g., opening the dialer, email client, or office details page).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL parameters and runtime API calls, without using localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. On initial load, the page controller reads type_of_issue, user_role, amount_in_dispute, and state from the query string of contact.html. However, these parameters are not applied to the form until getContactFormOptions completes. After getContactFormOptions returns, the controller compares the URL parameter values to the option lists provided in issue_types, user_roles, preferred_contact_methods, and states. For each field: if the parameter value exactly matches one of the option values, the form field is initialized to that option; otherwise, the field remains unselected. The amount_in_dispute parameter is parsed as a number and used to initialize the numeric field when parsing succeeds; otherwise, it is ignored. The in-page form state (the pending ContactInquiry) remains in memory only for the current page session and is not written to URL parameters or localStorage unless explicitly chosen for deep-linking (for example, updating type_of_issue or state in the URL when a user changes them). Any such URL updates should use standard browser history APIs but must stay consistent with the currently selected options. After a successful submitContactInquiry call, the page state includes the returned contact_inquiry and confirmation_message; the presence of this state can be used to control whether the form is shown, locked, or hidden and to ensure that reloading the page without the response data (e.g., via a fresh navigation) starts a new, empty inquiry.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page behavior: parses URL parameters, calls getContactFormOptions, initializes the pending ContactInquiry state, and controls whether the form and confirmation panel are shown.

Encapsulates the call to getContactFormOptions, manages loading and error states for form option data, and exposes the loaded options (issue_types, user_roles, preferred_contact_methods, states) to child components such as the contact form.

Renders and manages the structured contact form. It binds each input field to the pending ContactInquiry instance, uses the options provided by contact-form-options-loader, applies URL-derived initial values supplied by the controller, performs client-side validation, and calls submitContactInquiry when the user submits a valid form.

Handles selection of the preferred_contact_method field using the options returned by getContactFormOptions. Ensures that the selected value is valid and updates the parent form's ContactInquiry state accordingly.

Displays a confirmation view after a successful submitContactInquiry call. Shows the confirmation_message and relevant fields from the returned contact_inquiry, and may provide basic next-step guidance. It is shown or hidden based on the presence of a successful submission result in page state.
