Optionally displays alternative contact methods such as office contact details based on Office data provided to the page (for example, server-rendered or passed as props). This component does not invoke any interfaces but allows users to initiate contact outside the structured form.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with content centered within a max-width container. Navigation uses horizontal flex-like distribution; hero content appears centered within the central portion of the grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the global structure from the mockup: a compact black utility header at the very top, a full-width deep red navigation bar immediately beneath it, and a large hero banner with a courthouse-style background image dominating the upper half of the viewport. Within that hero, the contact page uses a strong two-line 'Contact Our Firm' headline and brief supporting text, centered. Directly below the hero, the main content area is a vertically stacked sequence: the form (with its internal preferred-contact-method subsection) as the primary block, followed by a slimmer alternative-contact information band that echoes the practice-areas strip treatment from the mockup. Non-visual controller/loader components are logically anchored to the top of the main content area and to the form region but do not introduce new visible sections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the global structure from the mockup: a compact black utility header at the very top, a full-width deep red navigation bar immediately beneath it, and a large hero banner with a courthouse-style background image dominating the upper half of the viewport. Within that hero, the contact page uses a strong two-line 'Contact Our Firm' headline and brief supporting text, centered. Directly below the hero, the main content area is a vertically stacked sequence: the form (with its internal preferred-contact-method subsection) as the primary block, followed by a slimmer alternative-contact information band that echoes the practice-areas strip treatment from the mockup. Non-visual controller/loader components are logically anchored to the top of the main content area and to the form region but do not introduce new visible sections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and red navigation together occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. The hero banner (with the courthouse image and contact intro copy) fills the next ~40–50%, leaving the remaining ~30–40% of the viewport for the contact form and its confirmation state. The form itself is centered in the content column and typically spans about 60–70% of the page width, leaving generous side gutters. The alternative-contact panel at the bottom acts as a thinner full-width band, about 10–15% of the viewport height, visually similar to the practice-areas strip but slightly taller to fit more information. Vertical spacing between hero and form follows the 24–32px step, and the form and alternative-contact panel each have 32–48px of internal padding to maintain the moderate density seen in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Consistency with the mockup means the top-of-page elements are compact and information-rich, while the key conversion area (the contact form and its confirmation state) gets slightly more breathing room but stays fairly dense and efficient. Fields in the form are stacked in a single column with 12–16px vertical gaps, and groupings (such as the preferred contact method selector) are separated with 24px top and bottom spacing. The alternative-contact panel is denser, with tightly packed lines of contact details and icons, similar in informational density to the practice-areas strip, but still organized for quick scanning.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow continues the mockup’s top-down Z-pattern. The eye begins at the logo in the black header, moves horizontally to the right-side contact details, then down to the centered red navigation. From there, the large white hero headline on the dark courthouse image pulls attention down to the center of the hero. A prominent, colored 'Start Your Consultation' CTA (anchoring the hero to the form) guides the eye further down to the contact form, which is the main focal block in the mid-lower portion of the page. Within the form, the user naturally moves top-to-bottom through fields, pausing at the visually distinct preferred-contact-method selector. After submission, the confirmation panel occupies the same visual region, becoming the main focus. Finally, the eye rests on the bottom alternative-contact panel, a strong horizontal band that mirrors the hero’s practice-areas strip and acts as a secondary call to action for those who prefer phone or in-person contact.

layout.overall_description: Viewed as a whole, the contact page feels like a formal, authoritative legal website that continues the mockup’s visual language. A black utility header and deep red navigation bar form a compact, information-dense top frame. Below, a dramatic, full-width courthouse hero spans most of the upper half of the screen, with a centered white 'Contact Our Firm' headline and brief reassurance copy floating over the darker portion of the image. Just beneath the hero, the layout transitions into a centered content column where the contact inquiry form dominates: a structured, single-column stack of labeled fields, framed by generous left and right gutters and about 32–48px of internal padding. Midway through the form, the preferred contact method selector stands out as a clearly grouped subsection, using a horizontal row of options that still respects the linear, stacked flow. When the form is submitted, this entire central block is replaced by a confirmation panel in the same position and width, maintaining the page’s visual rhythm. At the bottom of the main content, a full-width alternative-contact band stretches edge-to-edge, echoing the hero’s bottom strip treatment: darker background, compact lines of phone, address, and office hours, arranged for quick scanning. The page reads top-to-bottom in a single column of full-width sections, with subtle transitions and a consistent traditional legal aesthetic tying everything together.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init controller is logically positioned at the very top of the main content area, directly beneath the hero section but before any visible form elements. Visually, it is non-rendering: it occupies zero height in the layout and does not alter spacing. Conceptually, it sits at the start of the central content column that will later contain the contact form and confirmation panel, ensuring that URL parameters and page-level state are resolved before anything user-facing appears. Because it is invisible, it does not affect the grid; the first visible element beneath the hero remains the contact form region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height element that executes before visible content within the normal document flow; it does not alter the positioning or spacing of other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader is associated directly with the contact form area, conceptually sitting just above the top edge of the form within the same centered content column. In its default, completed state, it is invisible and occupies no vertical space, allowing the form to appear immediately below the hero with a 24–32px gap. While loading, a subtle overlay or inline status indicator can appear: a small centered spinner and 'Loading form options…' line positioned in the middle of the form’s allotted space, approximately 40–50% down from the top of the viewport, aligned with the center of the 8–10 column-wide content column. This preserves the overall structure: the hero remains intact above, and the rest of the page below is simply pushed down by the temporary loading container, which matches the eventual form width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in the normal flow just above the form; typically zero-height, but when in a loading state, it temporarily occupies the same central width as the form and pushes subsequent content downward without overlapping the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact inquiry form is the primary content block immediately following the hero. It begins about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the hero image, centered horizontally within the page’s max-width container. On desktop, the form itself occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width (about 8 out of 12 implicit grid columns), with equal left and right gutters ensuring it feels substantial yet still framed by whitespace. Vertically, the form extends as a single, uninterrupted column of fields that typically fills at least one full screen height, depending on field count. Each field label and input pair is left-aligned, with 12–16px vertical spacing between fields and 24px separations between logical field groups. The overall form block has 32–48px padding on all sides, and a subtle, slightly lighter or darker background panel (depending on theme) to distinguish it from the underlying hero and the page background. The main submit button sits at the bottom of the form’s content stack, right-aligned or center-aligned within the form’s width, using a high-contrast color (akin to the cyan CTAs from the hero) to pull the eye downward. The form’s top aligns roughly with the point where the hero’s central CTAs would be on other pages, maintaining a sense of continuity with the mockup’s visual rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, anchored beneath the hero. It does not scroll independently; instead, the page scrolls as a whole, and on submission the form region is replaced by the confirmation panel in the same spatial footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The preferred-contact-method selector lives as an internal subsection of the main contact form, roughly in the middle of the form’s vertical stack. It appears after the basic personal information fields (such as name and email) and before detailed case description fields. Horizontally, it spans the full width of the form’s inner content area (matching the width of other fields), maintaining alignment with the form’s left edge. The section begins with a bold, all-caps or small-caps label aligned to the left, mirroring the authoritative typography of the navigation bar. Below the label, the contact method options (e.g., Phone, Email, Video Call) are displayed as a horizontal row of segmented controls or pill-shaped buttons, each occupying equal width across the form’s column. On larger screens, the options sit in a single row with 8–12px horizontal gaps; on narrower screens, they wrap to multiple rows while preserving a tight, organized pattern. Vertical padding above and below this subsection is slightly increased (about 24px) relative to regular fields, giving it a distinct visual grouping without breaking the overall dense, linear flow of the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully contained within the form’s standard column layout; it scrolls with the form and does not have independent fixed or sticky behavior. Its position remains relative to neighboring form fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation panel occupies exactly the same central content region as the contact form once a submission is successful. It appears in place of the form, starting at the same vertical offset beneath the hero (about 24–32px from the bottom of the hero image) and spanning the same 60–70% width of the page within the max-width container. The panel is styled as a prominent but contained block: a dark, refined background (deep charcoal or muted deep red) with 32–48px of internal padding on all sides. A clear, strong headline such as 'Thank You for Contacting Our Firm' is centered or left-aligned at the top of the panel, set in large, light-colored type that echoes the hero typography. Below it, concise explanatory text and next steps are stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing between lines. Any reference number or follow-up details are aligned in a neat, legal-document-like structure to maintain the corporate tone. The overall height of the panel is shorter than the full form in many cases, so additional vertical whitespace appears below it before the alternative-contact panel, allowing the confirmation to feel focused and self-contained. The panel gives the impression of a formal receipt or acknowledgment sitting squarely in the mid-lower portion of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Hidden by default; when active, it replaces the form within the same layout slot in the normal document flow, without changing the position of the hero above or the alternative-contact panel below. It scrolls with the page as a static block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The alternative-contact panel is presented as the final major section of the page, directly beneath the form or confirmation panel. It begins with a 24–32px vertical gap from the component above, then stretches full-width across the viewport, echoing the hero’s bottom practice-areas strip but with slightly more height to accommodate richer content. The background is a solid deep red or near-black tone, creating a strong horizontal band that visually anchors the bottom of the main content area. Within this band, content is centered inside the same max-width container as the form. On desktop, the information is laid out in one or two horizontal rows: for example, a left-aligned column listing 'Call Us Now' with a large phone number, a middle column with office address and a subtle location icon, and a right column with office hours and perhaps a link to directions or a map. These columns are tightly spaced, with 16–24px between columns and 8–12px between lines, reflecting the denser, utility-like feel of the mockup’s practice-areas strip. On mobile, these stack vertically but maintain the solid band aesthetic. The panel’s height is modest—roughly 10–15% of the viewport—so it reads as a compact but important secondary option for users who prefer immediate contact or in-person visits.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width section at the bottom of the main content, following the normal document flow. It does not stick or float; instead, it serves as the final, anchored band users encounter as they scroll down the page.

name: Locations & Offices
title: Locations & Offices
description: Find law firm office locations by entering an address or ZIP code, applying distance and weekend hours filters, and viewing sortable results with links to office detail pages.

core_features: Search for offices using an origin input (address or ZIP code) and optional filters for distance radius and weekend hours, powered by the searchOffices interface.
Synchronize search criteria and results state with URL parameters (origin_input, distance_radius_miles, has_weekend_hours, sort_by) so searches are shareable and restorable.
Display a list of matching offices returned by searchOffices, including key details such as address, contact information, hours summary, and distance when available.
Allow users to sort results using the sort_by parameter (for example to prioritize nearest offices) and re-query searchOffices when sort order changes.
Provide navigation from each office result card to the corresponding office detail page (office.html) while preserving the current origin_input in the URL.
user_workflows:
User arrives with no URL parameters:
1. locations.html loads with empty or default URL parameter values.
2. The URL state manager reads URL parameters and detects that origin_input is missing or empty.
3. The page displays the search form with blank origin input and default filter controls (no searchOffices call is made yet).
4. The user enters an origin input and optionally adjusts distance radius and weekend hours.
5. The user submits the search form.
6. The form submission handler updates the browser URL to locations.html with origin_input and any selected filter/sort parameters.
7. The page logic (or router) reloads/updates, the URL state manager reads the new parameters, and searchOffices is called with these parameters.
8. The Office array returned from searchOffices is bound to the results list component, which renders clickable office cards.

User arrives from another page with origin_input pre-filled:
1. User clicks a "Locations" link elsewhere in the site that navigates to locations.html with an origin_input parameter.
2. On load, the URL state manager parses origin_input and any existing distance_radius_miles, has_weekend_hours, and sort_by parameters.
3. The search form fields are pre-filled based on these URL values.
4. searchOffices is called immediately with the parsed parameters.
5. When results are returned, the results list and toolbar display the matching offices and current sort/filter state.
6. The user can adjust filters (e.g., toggle weekend hours or change distance radius) and submit again, which updates the URL and re-calls searchOffices.

User refines results and changes sort order:
1. User starts with a set of results already displayed (after a prior searchOffices call).
2. The user changes the sort option in the results toolbar (e.g., selects a different sort_by value).
3. The sort change handler updates the sort_by parameter in the URL while preserving origin_input, distance_radius_miles, and has_weekend_hours.
4. The URL update triggers a new call to searchOffices with the updated sort_by value.
5. The updated Office array is re-bound to the results list, displaying offices in the new order.

User filters for offices with weekend hours:
1. User has already executed a search and sees results.
2. The user toggles the weekend hours filter control.
3. The filter change handler updates has_weekend_hours in the URL query string (adding, changing, or removing it depending on the toggle state) while keeping existing origin_input, distance_radius_miles, and sort_by.
4. searchOffices is called again with the updated has_weekend_hours value.
5. The results list updates to show only offices that meet the weekend hours criterion returned by searchOffices.

User navigates to an office detail page and back:
1. From the results list, the user clicks on an office card.
2. The office card click handler navigates to office.html with office_id set to the selected office's id and origin_input set to the current origin_input from the URL.
3. The user views details on office.html.
4. When the user returns to locations.html (e.g., via browser back or a "Back to locations" link), the URL still contains the previous search parameters.
5. The URL state manager on locations.html reads the existing parameters and calls searchOffices with the same values, restoring the previous results and search state.

User experiences empty results or errors:
1. User submits a search and the URL is updated.
2. searchOffices is called with the given parameters.
3. If searchOffices returns an empty array, the empty state component is shown instead of the office list, while still displaying the search form and filters.
4. If searchOffices fails (e.g., network error), an error state component is shown, with an action to retry the search (which re-calls searchOffices using the same URL parameters).
interactions: Typing in the origin input field updates the local form state for origin_input without immediately calling searchOffices.
Changing the distance radius control updates local form state for distance_radius_miles; the new radius only affects searchOffices after form submission.
Toggling the weekend hours filter updates local form state for has_weekend_hours and visually reflects the current selection.
Submitting the search form constructs a URL query string with origin_input, distance_radius_miles, has_weekend_hours, and sort_by, navigates to that URL, and triggers a new searchOffices call.
Changing the sort control updates the sort_by parameter in the URL (while preserving other parameters) and triggers a new searchOffices call with the updated sort_by.
Clicking an office result card triggers navigation to office.html with office_id equal to the selected Office.id and origin_input equal to the current URL origin_input.
Clicking a retry action in an error state re-invokes searchOffices using the current URL parameters.

Page load or URL change events cause the state manager to parse URL parameters, derive the current search criteria, and conditionally call searchOffices (only when origin_input is present and non-empty).
page_functionality.state_logic:
On each page load or URL change, the Locations & Offices page reads the URL parameters origin_input, distance_radius_miles, has_weekend_hours, and sort_by. If origin_input is present and not empty, the page calls the searchOffices interface with these values (after parsing and converting types as needed). If distance_radius_miles is missing or cannot be parsed as a number, it is omitted from the searchOffices call so the interface can apply its default behavior. If has_weekend_hours is missing, it is treated as undefined and omitted from the call; if provided as a string representing a boolean, it is converted to a boolean for searchOffices. If sort_by is missing, it is omitted from the call so the interface can use its own default sort behavior.

When the user submits the search form or changes filters/sort options, the page does not use localStorage. Instead, it updates the query parameters in the locations.html URL to reflect the new state (origin_input, distance_radius_miles, has_weekend_hours, sort_by). Updating the URL either reloads the page or triggers client-side routing; in either case, the URL state manager reads the updated parameters and calls searchOffices with the current values.

The Office array returned by searchOffices is the single source of truth for the results list. The page reads any distance_miles values from each Office and uses them for display and sorting feedback, but sorting logic itself is delegated to searchOffices via the sort_by parameter. If searchOffices returns offices without distance_miles populated, the UI suppresses distance-specific display elements without attempting to compute distances locally.

No persistent state is stored outside the URL and in-memory page state; closing and reopening the page without preserving the URL will reset the search. Sharing the URL preserves the full search criteria, allowing others to load the same search by visiting the URL, which will re-run searchOffices with the encoded parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes and maintains the page's functional state by reading URL parameters, deriving current search criteria, and orchestrating calls to the searchOffices interface. It exposes parsed state to other components (search form, filters, toolbar, results list) and re-executes searchOffices whenever the URL changes in a way that affects search parameters.
event_handlers: onInit: Parses the current URL query string to extract origin_input, distance_radius_miles, has_weekend_hours, and sort_by. If origin_input is present and non-empty, calls searchOffices with these parameters and stores the returned Office array in shared state. If origin_input is missing or empty, initializes shared state with no results and flags that no search has been run yet.

onUrlChange: Listens for browser navigation events that change query parameters (e.g., back/forward, internal link navigation). Re-parses parameters and re-calls searchOffices when origin_input is present and non-empty, updating the shared Office array.

onSearchCriteriaChange: Receives new search criteria from the search form or toolbar components and updates the browser URL accordingly without directly calling searchOffices (the subsequent URL change triggers onUrlChange, which calls searchOffices).

components.functionality: Captures user input for origin_input and main search filters, including distance radius and weekend hours preference. It initializes its fields from the current URL parameters and, on submission, updates the URL to reflect the user's chosen criteria.
event_handlers: onInit: Reads current URL parameters (via the state manager) and pre-fills the origin input field, distance radius control, and weekend hours toggle to match origin_input, distance_radius_miles, and has_weekend_hours.
onChangeOriginInput: Updates local form state when the user modifies the origin input; does not immediately update URL or call searchOffices.
onChangeDistanceRadius: Updates local form state for distance_radius_miles when the user changes the radius selection; does not immediately update URL or call searchOffices.
onToggleWeekendHours: Updates local form state for has_weekend_hours when the user toggles the weekend hours filter; does not immediately update URL or call searchOffices.

onSubmit: Prevents the default form submission behavior, validates origin_input (e.g., ensures it is not empty), and sends a structured search criteria object to the URL state manager. The criteria object includes origin_input, distance_radius_miles (only if valid), has_weekend_hours (only if explicitly set), and sort_by (preserved from current URL or local state). The state manager then updates the URL to locations.html with these parameters, which indirectly triggers a new searchOffices call.

components.functionality: Displays the current search summary (for example, indicating that results are being shown for a given origin_input and filters) and provides controls to adjust sort order and, optionally, refine filters without leaving the results context. It keeps the filter and sort controls synchronized with URL parameters.
event_handlers: onInit: Reads current URL parameters to set the initial state of the sort control and any duplicated filter toggles, ensuring the toolbar matches the search form and the active search criteria.

onChangeSort: When the user selects a new sort option, constructs an updated parameter set by changing only sort_by while preserving origin_input, distance_radius_miles, and has_weekend_hours. Delegates to the URL state manager to update the URL, which triggers re-execution of searchOffices.

onToggleWeekendHoursShortcut: Optionally provides a weekend-hours toggle in the toolbar; when changed, updates has_weekend_hours via the URL state manager similarly to the main search form, causing searchOffices to run again.

components.functionality: Renders the list of office search results returned by searchOffices. It listens for updates to the shared Office array from the URL state manager and re-renders when new results arrive. If there are no results, it hides the list and allows the empty-state component to render instead.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: Receives the latest Office array from the state manager after each searchOffices call and re-renders the list of office cards. If the array is empty, triggers display of the empty state component instead of the list.

onClickOfficeCard: When a user clicks a specific office card, reads that Office.id and the current origin_input from the URL, then navigates to office.html with office_id set to the selected id and origin_input set to the current origin_input.

components.functionality: Represents a single office within the results list. Displays key fields from the Office model such as name, address elements, contact information, office_hours summary, has_weekend_hours status, and distance_miles (when provided). The entire card is interactive and navigates to the office detail page on click.
event_handlers: onInit: Receives an Office instance from the results list component, binds its fields to the card display, and conditionally shows distance-related information only if distance_miles is defined.

onClick: When the user clicks anywhere within the card (or on a dedicated "View details" control), reads Office.id and the current origin_input (from URL or shared state) and performs a navigation to office.html with query parameters office_id and origin_input as defined in the outgoing navigation configuration.

components.functionality: Manages and displays the loading state of office search results while searchOffices is in progress. It prevents multiple overlapping calls from being triggered by disabling relevant controls until the current request completes or fails.
event_handlers: onSearchStart: Triggered by the state manager immediately before calling searchOffices. Sets an internal loading flag to true, which can be used to display a loading indicator and disable form submission and sort controls.
onSearchSuccess: Triggered when searchOffices successfully returns an Office array. Clears the loading flag so controls are re-enabled and hides the loading indicator.
onSearchError: Triggered when searchOffices fails. Clears the loading flag and allows the error-state component to render an appropriate message.

components.functionality: Displays contextual messaging when searchOffices returns an empty Office array for the current search criteria. It offers actions to adjust filters or clear certain parameters to broaden the search.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: Listens to changes in the Office array from the state manager. If the array length is zero and a search has been executed (origin_input is present), shows the empty-state messaging.

onClickAdjustFilters: When the user clicks an action to adjust filters, focuses the search form and may optionally reset selected parameters (e.g., distance_radius_miles or has_weekend_hours) by sending updated criteria to the URL state manager, which updates the URL and triggers a new searchOffices call.

components.functionality: Handles and displays errors encountered when calling searchOffices, including network or server issues. It provides a retry mechanism that preserves current search criteria.
event_handlers: onSearchError: Receives error notifications from the state manager when searchOffices fails. Sets an internal error flag and stores minimal error context for display.
onClickRetry: When the user clicks a retry action, clears the error flag and instructs the state manager to re-call searchOffices using the current URL parameters without modifying them.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with content centered within a max-width container. Navigation uses horizontal flex-like distribution; hero content appears centered within the central portion of the grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global black header and red navigation, the Locations page uses a tall hero-like search section that mirrors the original hero treatment. At the center of this hero, the locations search form sits where the original tagline and CTAs were, framed by ample architectural background imagery. Directly beneath this hero search section, a compact results toolbar spans the content width, followed by a vertically stacked list of location cards. Status states (loading, empty, error) occupy the same results region, each replacing the list when active. All content is arranged in a straightforward top-to-bottom sequence with no sidebars, aligned to an implicit 12-column centered grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global black header and red navigation, the Locations page uses a tall hero-like search section that mirrors the original hero treatment. At the center of this hero, the locations search form sits where the original tagline and CTAs were, framed by ample architectural background imagery. Directly beneath this hero search section, a compact results toolbar spans the content width, followed by a vertically stacked list of location cards. Status states (loading, empty, error) occupy the same results region, each replacing the list when active. All content is arranged in a straightforward top-to-bottom sequence with no sidebars, aligned to an implicit 12-column centered grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header and navigation together consume the top ~15–20% of the viewport, unchanged from the mockup. The hero search section beneath them takes roughly 40–50% of the viewport height on initial load, using generous vertical padding (48–64px top and bottom inside the hero content band) to echo the original hero. Below that, the results toolbar and results list share the remaining 30–40% and extend vertically as needed. Each office card occupies nearly the full content width within the max-width container, with moderate vertical spacing (16–24px) between cards. Status states are given similar vertical space as the list region, centered within the content area but not full-screen blocking, aligning with the information-first, non-dramatic tone.
layout_strategies.content_density: The search hero is moderately spacious, with a dense but neatly grouped form centered in a middle band, reflecting the mockup’s balance of gravitas and readability. Inputs, dropdowns, and buttons are packed efficiently with 8–16px internal spacing, avoiding airy minimalism while still feeling orderly. The results toolbar is compact and utility-driven, with filters and sort controls placed tightly but clearly, using 8–12px gaps. The results list is denser: cards are stacked with modest 16–24px separation and structured content lines (name, address, distance, hours, links) with minimal extra whitespace, mirroring how the header and nav prioritize information over decoration.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow maintains the mockup’s top-down Z pattern: users start at the global logo and top-right utility elements, move through the bold red navigation, and then are pulled down into the center of the hero by a strong, all-caps page title and the prominent search form. From there, the eye moves slightly downward to the results toolbar strip, which acts like a pivot between search intent and detailed information. The eye then continues down through the vertical stack of location cards. When active, status states (loading, empty, error) sit precisely where results would appear, so the user’s flow—from search to feedback—is uninterrupted and predictable.

layout.overall_description: Under the existing black header and deep red navigation bar, the Locations page opens into a tall, dramatic hero area that continues the courthouse or architectural imagery from the mockup, slightly darkened to let white text and controls stand out. Centered within this hero is a solid, rectangular search panel aligned to the middle of the page, spanning roughly 60–70% of the content width. It carries the page title, a concise subtitle, and the locations search form, stacked vertically with clear but tight spacing. The form’s main inputs and primary search button sit in a single horizontal row on desktop, echoing the two-button CTA pairing from the original hero, while secondary options like distance or weekend filters align just beneath in a tighter row. At the bottom edge of this hero section, a thin, full-width strip of small, all-caps links (for example, quick links to major cities or “View all offices”) mimics the practice-areas band from the mockup and visually anchors the hero. Below this, the page transitions into a white or very light content section. A horizontal results toolbar sits immediately under the hero, spanning the full content width inside the centered max-width container, with sort and filter controls aligned left and right. Beneath the toolbar, location result cards stack vertically, each nearly full-width in the grid, with subtle dividing lines and consistent padding to maintain a formal, corporate feel. When results are being loaded or if no offices match the filters—or if there’s an error—those messages appear centered within the same results area, using the same width and alignment as the cards, ensuring that feedback feels integrated rather than disruptive. The entire layout feels disciplined and authoritative, with a clear top-to-bottom narrative from search intent to findable office details.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visible, behavioral component that does not occupy any visual space on the page. Conceptually, it sits behind the main content, managing synchronization of the search form, filters, and results with the URL. It does not affect positioning or spacing of visible components; instead, it ensures that when the user changes the search parameters, the visible search form, results toolbar, and results list update in place within the existing layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Not rendered visually; operates purely in the background without layout impact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The locations search form is the centerpiece of a hero-like section directly beneath the global navigation. It is contained within a centered panel that sits in the middle vertical band of the hero, occupying roughly 60–70% of the max-width content area and about 40–50% of the hero height. The panel’s top line is a strong, all-caps page title (e.g., “FIND A LOCATION”) centered horizontally, with a single-line subtitle below it in smaller, lighter text. Below the subtitle, the form controls are arranged in a vertical stack of one or two rows. The first row features the primary input fields (e.g., address/ZIP) and a prominent primary search button, aligned on a single horizontal line on desktop: address/ZIP field taking about 60% of the row width on the left, an optional distance dropdown taking ~20% in the middle, and the search button (in a high-contrast color like cyan or gold) occupying the remaining ~20% on the right. Elements in this row have consistent horizontal padding (~24–32px) and vertical padding (~12–16px) inside their input/button borders. A second row just beneath, left-aligned with the first, contains secondary options such as a weekend-hours toggle and possibly a checkbox for “Show only offices with weekend hours,” spaced with 8–16px between controls. Around the form panel, there is generous vertical breathing room inside the hero—roughly 48–64px padding above the title and another 48–64px below the lowest control—so the architectural background remains visible. At the very bottom edge of this hero section, visually tied to the form, runs a slim horizontal strip containing small, all-caps quick links (e.g., major cities or state names) centered across the width, echoing the practice-areas strip in the original mockup. On smaller screens, the form stack collapses to a single-column list, with each input and button spanning nearly 100% of the content width and 16–24px vertical spacing between elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; remains at the top of the scrollable content area and scrolls away normally as the user moves down to results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results toolbar sits immediately below the hero search section, separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space. It forms a thin, full-width horizontal bar inside the same centered max-width content container, visually functioning like the navigation bar for the results. The toolbar height is compact—around 48–64px—and its background is a subtle light shade (or white with a bottom border) to distinguish it from the hero above and the cards below. Within the toolbar, content is arranged in a left-right distribution: on the left, a line of text indicating the number of locations found and the current search area (e.g., “12 OFFICES NEAR BOSTON, MA”), set in small caps or bold to echo the authoritative style; on the right, a horizontal cluster of filter controls such as a sort-by dropdown and a toggle for weekend hours, spaced tightly with 8–12px gaps. Everything is vertically center-aligned within the bar. On smaller screens, the left summary moves to the top of the toolbar and the filters drop below it in a second row, all left-aligned, still within the same full-width band. The toolbar visually bridges the hero and the results list, creating a clear logical transition from searching to browsing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Optionally sticky on desktop and tablet: when the user scrolls the results list, the toolbar remains fixed at the top of the viewport within the content column, ensuring continuous access to filters; on mobile, it may scroll normally to avoid occupying excessive vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The office results list occupies the entire content area below the results toolbar, extending downward as many screens as necessary. It is contained within the same centered max-width container, starting about 16–24px below the toolbar. The list is a simple vertical stack of office cards, each card spanning roughly 100% of the available content width within the 12-column grid (leaving the standard site margins on both sides). Between each card is a consistent vertical gap of about 16–24px, with the list’s left edge aligned precisely with the left edge of the toolbar content, maintaining a clean visual column. There are no side gutters for filters; all filtering happens via the toolbar above. At the very bottom of the list, there is sufficient bottom padding (48–64px) before any site-level footer to avoid a cramped feeling. On very wide screens, the list remains centered and constrained in width instead of stretching full-viewport, preserving the formal, controlled layout style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scroll-based; the list extends vertically and scrolls under any sticky behavior of the results toolbar, with no overlapping elements. It dynamically shows or hides depending on loading/empty/error states managed by the URL state manager.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each office card is a rectangular block within the results list, nearly full-width inside the content container and aligned to the grid. The card uses a light background (white or very pale) with a subtle 1px border or faint shadow to distinguish it from the page background, echoing the formal card treatment prevalent on corporate sites. Internally, the card follows a structured, left-aligned layout. At the top-left is the office name in a bold, slightly larger type, possibly all caps, occupying the full card width. Directly beneath, the address is displayed over one or two lines, using standard sentence case and slightly smaller text. Below the address, a horizontal band presents secondary details: distance from the searched location on the left (e.g., “3.2 MILES”), office phone number, and key hours information (e.g., “OPEN TODAY UNTIL 6 PM” or “WEEKEND HOURS AVAILABLE”), separated by small vertical dividers or with 8–12px horizontal gaps. At the bottom of the card, aligned right, a primary link or button such as “VIEW OFFICE DETAILS” is presented in a high-contrast style that matches the site’s CTA language, with 24–32px horizontal and 12–16px vertical padding. If there’s a secondary action (e.g., “Get Directions”), it appears to the left of the primary action in a lower-emphasis style (link or outlined button), arranged horizontally. The card’s vertical padding is around 16–24px top and bottom, leading to a moderately dense but readable block. On mobile, the card content collapses into a single column: actions stack beneath the textual details, full-width, with 12–16px spacing between text blocks and actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a repeated block within the vertical list; no special behavior beyond responsive reflow from multi-column detail bands to single-column stacks on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading state occupies the same spatial region as the office results list, directly beneath the results toolbar. When active, instead of cards, a centered loading indicator appears, positioned roughly one-third down from the top of the visible results area so it is immediately noticeable after the user triggers a search. The indicator might be a simple spinner paired with a concise line of text (e.g., “SEARCHING NEARBY OFFICES…”), both centered horizontally within the content container. The overall width matches the results list column, with generous horizontal margins consistent with the grid. Vertical padding around the loading message is about 48–64px, giving it breathing room but not full-screen dominance. If skeleton placeholders are used, they mimic the dimensions and stacking of 2–4 office cards, filling the width and appearing in place of actual cards, preserving the expected card rhythm while data loads.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the results list, empty state, and error state; it shows only while data is being fetched, then smoothly transitions to the appropriate results representation without shifting the surrounding toolbar or hero layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state appears directly beneath the results toolbar, in the same location and width that the results list would occupy. The content is centered within the content container but left-aligned text-wise, matching the professional tone. At the top of the empty state block is a short, bold headline like “NO OFFICES FOUND WITH THESE CRITERIA,” occupying a single line across the column width. Below it, in smaller, regular text, are one or two lines explaining the situation and offering suggestions (e.g., “Try expanding your distance range or removing the weekend hours filter.”), with about 8–16px spacing between lines. Under this, there is a subtle, left-aligned button or link styled similarly to a secondary CTA (e.g., “CLEAR FILTERS” or “VIEW ALL LOCATIONS”), aligning with the authoritative yet helpful aesthetic. The entire empty-state block has vertical padding of roughly 48–64px before any footer, avoiding a sense of blankness while remaining compact compared to the hero. The background remains the standard page background (no full-screen overlay), ensuring the state feels integrated into the normal results area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the results list content while maintaining the toolbar and hero above it; it scrolls like normal content and does not alter header or toolbar behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state is placed in the same layout slot as the results list and empty state, immediately below the results toolbar and occupying the same content width. A narrow but visually distinct notice panel appears toward the top of this region, spanning the full width of the content column. The panel has a slightly stronger background shade (e.g., a muted red or warm gray) with a thin, darker border to match the formal legal aesthetic. Inside, a concise error headline such as “WE’RE UNABLE TO LOAD LOCATIONS RIGHT NOW” is aligned left, with a short explanatory sentence below it in smaller text. To the right on desktop, or below on mobile, there is a clear action such as a “RETRY SEARCH” button, styled with medium emphasis (not as bold as the hero CTAs but more prominent than plain links). Vertical padding inside this panel is about 16–24px, maintaining consistency with card padding, and a margin of about 24–32px separates it from any subsequent guidance text or fallback links (e.g., a link to a general contact page). The error state does not darken or overlay the entire page; instead, it presents itself as a serious but contained message within the normal results area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shown in place of the results list and empty/loading states when an error occurs, while leaving the hero and toolbar intact for quick adjustments or retries. It scrolls with the page and does not fix itself to the viewport.

name: Office Detail & Directions
title: Office Details and Directions
description: Displays detailed information for a specific office location and provides an integrated directions workflow, including origin input, step-by-step route instructions, printing, and navigation to other nearby offices.

core_features: Load and display full office details (name, complete address, contact information, office_hours including weekend indicators, coordinates, distance_miles, is_primary) using the getOfficeDetail interface based on the office_id URL parameter.
Show a concise office summary panel with click-to-call and click-to-email actions derived from the Office record returned by getOfficeDetail.
Display office hours, including weekend availability highlights driven by has_weekend_hours, open_saturday, and open_sunday from the Office data.
Provide a map preview section that uses the office’s latitude and longitude for visual context and to support external map application deep links.

Expose a directions panel where users can enter or confirm an origin_input (ZIP or address), optionally pre-filled from the origin_input URL parameter, and then generate directions to the office using getOfficeDirections.
Render the step-by-step route_instructions returned by getOfficeDirections, with the ability to expand/collapse the full route details within the page.
Enable printing of directions via a "Print Directions" action that opens or renders the printable_html returned by getOfficeDirections in a print-friendly context.

When an originInputUsedForDirections value exists (either from the URL or user input), allow users to search for nearby offices using searchOffices and provide a "View Other Nearby Offices" navigation link to locations.html with origin_input propagated in the URL.
Maintain URL-based state (office_id and origin_input) so that direct links to this page open the correct office and optionally restore the user’s most recent directions origin context.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View office details
1) User arrives on office.html with a URL containing office_id.
2) The page reads office_id from the URL query string.
3) The page calls getOfficeDetail(office_id) and waits for the response.
4) Once office data loads, the office summary, address, contact info, office hours, and map preview components bind to the Office object and render their respective fields.
5) The user can use the call/email links directly from this view without interacting with directions or other offices.

Workflow: Get directions to an office
1) User is on office.html with office_id in the URL; origin_input may or may not be present.
2) If origin_input is present in the URL, the directions panel pre-fills its origin input field with that value; otherwise the field remains empty for user entry.
3) The user types or edits the origin input and submits the directions form (e.g., clicks "Get Directions").
4) The page calls getOfficeDirections(office_id, origin_inputEnteredInForm).
5) On success, the directions panel stores the returned OfficeDirections object (including route_instructions and printable_html) in page state and updates the URL query string’s origin_input to match the origin_input used for this request.
6) The directions panel displays the step-by-step route instructions; the user can expand or collapse the full list if the component supports condensed display.

Workflow: Print directions
1) After completing the directions workflow and having a valid OfficeDirections object, the user clicks the "Print Directions" action in the directions panel.
2) The component uses office_directions.printable_html to either open a dedicated print view (e.g., new window or overlay) or to populate a print container within the page.
3) The browser’s print dialog is triggered for the print view containing printable_html.
4) The user prints or cancels printing; the page state and URL remain unchanged after the print action.

Workflow: View other nearby offices based on user origin
1) User has already generated directions at least once, or arrived with origin_input present in the URL.
2) The directions panel exposes the currently effective originInputUsedForDirections, which is either the origin_input URL parameter on load or the most recently used origin_input from a successful getOfficeDirections call.
3) When an originInputUsedForDirections exists, the nearby offices panel triggers searchOffices(origin_input = originInputUsedForDirections, distance_radius_miles = value determined by site defaults or user input if provided, has_weekend_hours and sort_by as determined by page logic or controls).
4) After searchOffices returns a list of Office objects, the nearby offices panel displays a subset or summary of these offices (e.g., names and distances) to suggest alternatives.
5) The user clicks a "View Other Nearby Offices" link within this panel.
6) The page navigates to locations.html with the URL parameter origin_input set to originInputUsedForDirections so that the Locations page can automatically search from exactly the same origin.

Workflow: Deep link with pre-filled origin
1) Another page (e.g., locations.html) links to office.html with both office_id and origin_input in the URL.
2) On load, office.html uses office_id to call getOfficeDetail and simultaneously uses origin_input to prefill the directions origin field.
3) The user reviews office details and immediately submits the pre-filled directions form if desired, triggering getOfficeDirections without retyping the origin.
4) If the user modifies origin_input and re-submits, the URL query string is updated to reflect the latest origin_input so that a refreshed or shared URL reproduces the same starting origin context.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load for a specific office
- Trigger: Browser loads office.html with office_id in the query string.
- Behavior: The page-init component reads office_id and calls getOfficeDetail(office_id). On success, all Office-bound components render using the returned data. On failure, an error state is shown instead of office details.

Interaction: Directions origin input change
- Trigger: User types into or edits the origin input field in the directions panel.
- Behavior: The component updates its internal origin input state as the user types; this state is only used for API calls once the user submits the form.

Interaction: Submit directions form
- Trigger: User submits the directions form (e.g., clicks "Get Directions").
- Behavior: The component validates that an origin has been provided and then calls getOfficeDirections(office_idFromURL, currentOriginInputFieldValue). While awaiting the response, the component may show a loading state in the directions area. On success, route_instructions and printable_html are bound to the directions display and print controls, and the page updates the URL’s origin_input query parameter to the submitted origin. On error, an error message is shown in the directions area.

Interaction: Expand/collapse directions
- Trigger: User clicks an "Expand Directions" or "Collapse Directions" control in the directions panel.
- Behavior: The component toggles a boolean expanded state that controls whether all route_instructions steps are displayed or only a condensed subset (e.g., first few steps). The underlying OfficeDirections data does not change.

Interaction: Print directions
- Trigger: User clicks a "Print Directions" action in the directions panel.
- Behavior: The component uses office_directions.printable_html to render a print-optimized view and invokes the browser’s print functionality. The user can print or close the print dialog; no API calls or state reset occur on print.

Interaction: Click-to-call office
- Trigger: User clicks a "Call" button or phone link in the office summary panel.
- Behavior: The component initiates a phone call action using the Office.phone value (e.g., via a tel: link). No API calls or page state updates are required.

Interaction: Click-to-email office
- Trigger: User clicks an "Email" button or link in the office summary panel.
- Behavior: The component opens the user’s default email client with the Office.email as the recipient address. No API calls or page state updates are required.

Interaction: Load nearby offices after origin available
- Trigger: The page obtains a valid originInputUsedForDirections either from the URL on load or after a successful getOfficeDirections call.
- Behavior: The nearby offices panel calls searchOffices(origin_input = originInputUsedForDirections, distance_radius_miles and other filters determined by the component). On success, the component binds the returned Office array to its listing view. On error, it shows an appropriate fallback message while preserving the ability to navigate to locations.html.

Interaction: Navigate to "View Other Nearby Offices"
- Trigger: User clicks the "View Other Nearby Offices" link in the nearby offices panel.
- Behavior: The component constructs a URL for locations.html that includes origin_input=originInputUsedForDirections in the query string and navigates the browser to that URL.

Interaction: Change office via external navigation
- Trigger: User clicks an office link from locations.html or a lawyer profile (lawyer_profile.html) that points to office.html with a different office_id.
- Behavior: On load (or URL change in a single-page setup), the page-init component detects the new office_id, re-calls getOfficeDetail with the new ID, resets Office-related components to loading or empty states, and keeps any origin_input from the URL intact for the new office’s directions panel.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven entirely by URL parameters and API responses, without using localStorage or other persistent browser storage.

URL parameters:
- office_id (required for core functionality):
  - On initial page load, the page reads office_id from the query string.
  - If office_id is missing or invalid, the page should display an appropriate error state instead of attempting to load office details.
  - Whenever office_id changes (e.g., via navigation from another page), the page re-invokes getOfficeDetail(office_id) and resets office-specific components (summary, hours, map, directions) to reflect the new office.

- origin_input (optional, controls directions context):
  - On page load, if origin_input is present in the URL, the directions panel pre-fills its origin input field using this value and makes it available as the initial originInputUsedForDirections.
  - If origin_input is absent, the directions origin field starts empty, and originInputUsedForDirections remains undefined until the user successfully requests directions.
  - After a successful getOfficeDirections call, the page updates the URL query string’s origin_input to the origin used in that call. This ensures that reloading the page or sharing the URL reproduces the user’s selected origin.
  - The nearby offices panel uses originInputUsedForDirections (which mirrors the latest effective origin_input) to decide when to call searchOffices and what value to pass as its origin_input parameter.

In-page derived state (non-persistent):
- officeData: Bound to the Office object returned from getOfficeDetail. It is cleared or set to a loading state whenever office_id changes.
- directionsData: Bound to the OfficeDirections object returned from getOfficeDirections. It is reset if office_id changes, since directions are specific to a particular office, and may also be cleared when the user intentionally resets the directions form.
- originInputFieldValue: The current value of the directions origin input field; initialized from origin_input (if present) on load and subsequently driven by user edits. It becomes the origin_input for the next getOfficeDirections call upon form submission.
- originInputUsedForDirections: The last successfully submitted origin_input value from a getOfficeDirections call, or the initial origin_input from the URL if directions have not yet been requested. This value is used for calling searchOffices and for setting the origin_input parameter when navigating to locations.html.
- directionsExpanded: A boolean used to control whether the full set of route_instructions is visible; toggled by the user and not reflected in the URL.
- nearbyOfficesList: An array of Office objects populated by searchOffices when originInputUsedForDirections is available. This is not persisted to the URL and is recomputed as needed.

The combination of these URL parameters and derived state ensures that the page is directly linkable and shareable (via office_id and origin_input), while still providing a responsive, interactive user experience driven by the three assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Initialize the Office Detail & Directions page by reading URL parameters, loading office details, and preparing the initial state for directions and nearby offices.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read office_id and optional origin_input from the URL query string. If office_id is present, call getOfficeDetail(office_id). Initialize originInputFieldValue and originInputUsedForDirections from origin_input if available.

onURLParamChange: When the URL changes to a different office_id (e.g., navigation within a single-page app), re-call getOfficeDetail with the new office_id, reset directionsData and nearbyOfficesList, and reinitialize originInputFieldValue from any updated origin_input parameter.
onOfficeDetailLoaded: After a successful getOfficeDetail response, store the Office object in officeData and notify dependent components (summary, hours, map, directions) to render.
onOfficeDetailError: If getOfficeDetail fails or returns no office, update page state to an error mode where dependent components show an appropriate fallback instead of office details.

components.functionality: Display key office information including name, full address, distance (if provided), and provide click-to-call and click-to-email actions.
event_handlers: onOfficeDataChange: When officeData is updated by the page-init component, render the name, address_line1, address_line2, city, state, zip, distance_miles, and any is_primary indicators.
onClickCallOffice: When the user clicks the call action, initiate a phone call using the Office.phone value (for example, by setting the browser location to a tel: URL).
onClickEmailOffice: When the user clicks the email action, open the default email client using Office.email as the recipient (for example, via a mailto: URL).

components.functionality: Present office hours information, highlighting weekend availability based on the Office record.
event_handlers: onOfficeDataChange: When officeData changes, parse and display office_hours in a structured format appropriate for the UI, and visually distinguish weekend availability using has_weekend_hours, open_saturday, and open_sunday.
onToggleHoursView: Optional handler to allow the user to expand/collapse detailed hours if the serialized office_hours contains per-day entries that can be hidden or shown.

components.functionality: Display a map preview for the office location and provide an action to open the location in an external maps application or service.
event_handlers: onOfficeDataChange: When officeData is available, use latitude and longitude (if provided) together with the address fields to render a map preview or construct a link to an external map service.
onClickOpenInMaps: When the user clicks an "Open in Maps" control, open an external map application or service using the office’s coordinates or formatted address.

components.functionality: Provide a form to input an origin, generate directions to the office using getOfficeDirections, display route instructions, and support printing and expanding the directions.
event_handlers: onInitFromURL: On initial render, if origin_input is present in the URL, initialize originInputFieldValue with that value so the user sees it pre-filled in the directions origin input.
onChangeOriginInput: Update originInputFieldValue as the user types in the origin input field; this state is local to the component until form submission.

onSubmitGetDirections: On form submission, validate that originInputFieldValue is not empty, then call getOfficeDirections(office_idFromURL, originInputFieldValue). Set a loading state while awaiting the response.

onGetOfficeDirectionsSuccess: When getOfficeDirections returns, store the OfficeDirections object in directionsData, derive originInputUsedForDirections from office_directions.origin_input, clear any previous errors, and update the URL’s origin_input query parameter to match this value.

onGetOfficeDirectionsError: If getOfficeDirections fails, show an error message in the directions area and leave previous directionsData (if any) untouched or clear it according to design requirements.
onToggleDirectionsExpanded: Toggle the directionsExpanded boolean to show or hide the full list of route_instructions steps. The component uses directionsExpanded to decide how many steps to display.

onClickPrintDirections: If directionsData is available, render office_directions.printable_html in a print-friendly context (such as a new window or print container) and invoke the browser’s print dialog.

components.functionality: When an origin for directions is known, search for and display other offices near that origin and provide navigation to the full Locations page with the origin propagated.
event_handlers: onOriginInputUsedForDirectionsChange: When originInputUsedForDirections becomes available or changes (after URL initialization or a successful getOfficeDirections call), trigger a searchOffices request using origin_input = originInputUsedForDirections and additional parameters such as distance_radius_miles and sort_by determined by component logic or user controls.

onSearchOfficesSuccess: When searchOffices returns an array of Office objects, bind this array to nearbyOfficesList and render a subset or list of nearby offices along with key information like name and distance_miles.
onSearchOfficesError: If searchOffices fails, display an appropriate fallback message while still allowing the user to navigate to locations.html via the "View Other Nearby Offices" link.

onClickViewOtherNearbyOffices: When the user clicks the link to view more offices, navigate to locations.html with origin_input set to originInputUsedForDirections in the query string so that the Locations page can run its own search using the same origin context.

components.functionality: Coordinate rendering order and visibility of all office-detail-related components (summary, hours, map, directions, nearby offices) based on officeData, directionsData, and URL-derived state.
event_handlers: onOfficeDataReady: Once officeData is loaded by the page-init component, ensure that office-summary-panel, office-hours-panel, and office-map-preview are displayed, and that the directions-panel is enabled and aware of the current office_id.

onDirectionsDataChange: When directionsData is updated by the directions-panel, notify the nearby-offices-panel about the new originInputUsedForDirections so it can decide whether to call searchOffices and show nearby offices.
onErrorOrMissingOffice: If the page-init component indicates that office details could not be loaded, hide components that depend on Office or show an error state container instead.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with content centered within a max-width container. Navigation uses horizontal flex-like distribution; hero content appears centered within the central portion of the grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing global header/navigation/hero, the page body continues the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a compact but information-rich office summary band at the top of the content area, paired horizontally with office hours; a dominant, full-width map preview section beneath; followed by a full-width directions workflow section; and finally a thin, full-width nearby offices strip acting as a visual anchor at the bottom. Each of these is a stacked, full-width section, with internal left–right structure where needed, consistent with the mockup’s lack of true sidebars.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing global header/navigation/hero, the page body continues the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a compact but information-rich office summary band at the top of the content area, paired horizontally with office hours; a dominant, full-width map preview section beneath; followed by a full-width directions workflow section; and finally a thin, full-width nearby offices strip acting as a visual anchor at the bottom. Each of these is a stacked, full-width section, with internal left–right structure where needed, consistent with the mockup’s lack of true sidebars.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the upper 20–25% of the page body is dedicated to the office summary + hours band; the map preview occupies the next 30–35% as a visually strong, image-driven area; the directions panel, being the main interactive workflow, uses another 30–35% with generous vertical padding around the form and instructions; and the nearby offices strip takes the bottom 5–10% as a narrow, dense band. Within sections, content stays within a centered max-width container (~70–80% of viewport width), with vertical spacing based on the 8px scale (24–32px between stacked blocks, 48–64px padding for the map and directions sections so they feel substantial).
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately dense, professional-service feel: the office summary and hours are tightly composed with limited whitespace, focusing on quick scanning of address, contact details, and opening times. The map and directions sections introduce more breathing room around key controls and headings—larger type, clear groupings—yet avoid overly airy spacing. Buttons and inputs follow the observed paddings (buttons ~24–32px horizontal / 12–16px vertical; section paddings and gaps in 8px multiples) to balance readability with a no-nonsense, corporate tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a top-down Z-pattern adapted from the mockup: users first anchor on the office name and address at the top-left of the content area, then sweep right to see office hours. From there, attention drops down to the bold, central map preview, then to the directions panel where the origin input field and main directions CTA sit in a high-contrast band near the top of that section. The eye then moves through the left-to-right directions workflow (input/options to step-by-step results) before finally resting on the dark nearby-offices strip at the bottom, which mirrors the hero’s practice-areas bar as a concluding navigational element.

layout.overall_description: Within the established global frame of a dark, authoritative header and navigation, the Office Detail & Directions page presents a vertically stacked sequence of full-width sections. At the top of the content, a compact, card-like band pairs the office’s name, address, and key contact CTAs on the left with a tightly organized hours panel on the right, both aligned within a centered grid. Below this, a broad, image-driven map preview stretches nearly the full width, echoing the hero’s dramatic imagery while keeping the office marker and map controls in the visual center. The next section deepens into the directions workflow: a prominent origin input and action buttons sit at the top of the panel, with step-by-step route details laid out clearly beneath, all framed by ample but controlled padding to suggest importance without feeling airy. Finally, a dark, narrow strip at the bottom lists nearby offices in a single line with bullet separators, functioning like the hero’s practice-area bar—a dense, low-profile navigator that visually anchors the page and invites further exploration of locations.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component acts as the structural wrapper for the office detail content area, beginning just below the global hero (or main page heading) and extending to the top of the global footer. It defines a centered content column based on the implicit 12-column grid, with a max-width container (approximately 70–80% of viewport width) and equal left and right margins. The top margin from the hero to this section follows the mockup’s section rhythm—around 24–32px—to create a clear but not oversized separation. Vertically, it stacks the subsequent panels (office summary + hours, map preview, directions, nearby offices) in order, ensuring consistent vertical gaps of 24–32px between these full-width sections and aligning all internal content to the same grid and baseline. Background remains a solid light-neutral or very dark-neutral consistent with the site’s body area, allowing the more visual map and CTA elements to stand out.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the standard document flow and grid container; no fixed or sticky behavior. All child components follow its stacking order and horizontal centering.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned at the top of the page body within the page-init wrapper, the office summary panel occupies the left two-thirds (approximately 7–8 of 12 columns) of a horizontal band, with the office-hours-panel aligned alongside it to the right. The panel sits about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the hero and extends nearly full width within the max-width container, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. The office name appears in a bold, all-caps or strong serif heading at the top-left, spanning most of the panel’s width; the address and contact details follow vertically beneath with relatively tight line spacing, reflecting the design’s information-dense character. Below the address block, two primary CTAs (e.g., “Call This Office” and “Get Directions”) are arranged horizontally, left-aligned, with each button sized generously (about 24–32px horizontal padding, 12–16px vertical) and spaced 16–24px apart, echoing the hero’s paired CTA pattern. On large screens, the panel’s height visually matches or slightly exceeds the adjacent hours panel, creating a cohesive, card-like band; on smaller screens, this panel stacks above the hours panel, each taking full width with a 16–24px gap between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline-block within a flex or grid row; left-aligned within its row. On desktop, shares a horizontal row with the hours panel; on tablet/mobile, becomes a full-width block stacked above the hours panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The office hours panel sits in the same horizontal band as the office-summary-panel, aligned to the right within the max-width container. On desktop, it occupies roughly the right one-third of the width (about 4–5 of 12 columns), forming a compact, vertically oriented card. Its top edge aligns with the top of the office summary heading, and its bottom edge aligns closely with the summary’s CTAs to maintain a unified bar-like appearance. Internally, the panel uses tighter vertical spacing (8–16px between rows) to list weekdays and corresponding hours in a clear, tabular format. A subtle top label (e.g., “Office Hours”) in small caps sits at the top-left of the panel, with the hours list beneath. Padding around the content is 16–24px, slightly less than the hero but consistent with the dense header/nav feel. On smaller viewports, this panel drops below the office summary, stretches to nearly full width, and maintains a 16–24px vertical gap from the summary above, ensuring a clearly separated yet still compact block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-aligned block within the same row as the office-summary-panel on larger screens; transitions to standard full-width block stacked below the summary on smaller screens. No sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the summary/hours band, the map preview occupies its own full-width section within the page-init wrapper, spanning the entire max-width container and visually echoing the hero’s large image treatment. A vertical gap of about 32px separates it from the band above. The map itself uses a fixed aspect ratio—approximately 16:9 or a height of 40–50% of the viewport on desktop—so it feels substantial and image-led without dominating the entire page. The map canvas runs nearly edge-to-edge within the container, with minimal top and bottom padding (16–24px) around it so the map feels integrated, not cramped. A simple, bold section title (e.g., “Office Location”) is left-aligned at the top of the section, just above the map, with tight spacing (8–12px) below, continuing the content-dense style. A marker showing the office location is visually centered within the initial viewport of the map, with any map controls clustered at top-left or bottom-right, keeping the center clear. On small screens, the map remains full width but the height is reduced slightly to preserve vertical space, while still clearly readable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard full-width section in document flow. The map area maintains a responsive fixed aspect ratio; no sticky or floating behavior relative to scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The directions panel is the main interactive section and sits immediately below the map preview, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the max-width container and includes generous vertical padding (approximately 48–64px top and bottom) to signal importance similar to the hero’s central band, while still using a relatively compact internal layout. At the top of the panel, a left-aligned section heading (e.g., “Get Directions to This Office”) runs across the upper portion, with a brief explanatory line just beneath in smaller type, both constrained to about 6–8 columns to avoid spanning too wide. Directly below this, a horizontal row contains the origin input field on the left (occupying roughly 60–70% of the row) and the primary “Get Directions” button on the right, mirroring the hero’s emphasis on a strong central CTA; secondary controls (mode selection, reverse route) can appear as smaller, right-aligned links or buttons adjacent to the main CTA, using the same spacing rules (8–16px gaps). 

Below this control row, the panel divides into two logical vertical stacks within the same section: on wider screens, the left two-thirds host the step-by-step route instructions, arranged as a vertically scrollable list with numbered steps and modest spacing (8–12px) between items, while the right one-third area can hold summary route info (distance, estimated time) and utility actions like “Print Directions” or “Send to Phone.” These actions are grouped in a tight vertical cluster near the top-right of the instructions area, aligned with the origin input row above to preserve the Z-pattern flow. On narrower screens, these elements stack: origin input row first, then route summary/actions, then step-by-step instructions full width, each separated by 16–24px. The overall background of this panel may be a slightly lighter or darker neutral block to distinguish it from the map above and tie back to the authoritative, formal tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard full-width section. Within the panel, the step-by-step instructions area can scroll internally if content is long while keeping the section header and origin input row visible in the viewport on taller screens, but there is no global sticky header; layout reflows to a single-column vertical stack on smaller devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This panel forms the bottom-most content section of the page body and visually echoes the hero’s practice-areas strip.
